title: Stories from Home
short_synopsis: A house can tell us much (or suspiciously little) about its inhabitants, and the drama of that dynamic is plumbed into cinema history. With this eclectic collection, we gather around the hearth for stories about home in all its manifestations and capacity for revelation. Come in—it’s an open house!
long_synopsis: Scores of films begin with a house. Whether cozily cluttered with oddments that draw in stories from all over the world, or airily spare, all right angles and suspiciously story-less, a house reveals a lot about a life. That relationship between a person and their home is rich with dramatic potential, one that filmmakers continue to mine—for tales of return and starting over, and ghost stories of every kind. For some film characters, homes are on loan. Tenanted, squatted in, or otherwise insecure, they flicker with the false promise of protection, as if afflicted with faulty electrics. Their resourceful residents find other earth on which to plant their feet: in networked communities or the social architecture of graffiti-caked neighborhoods. Spaces of illusion and longing, provisional homes are inherently cinematic. So, too, is a family home passed down through generations. These old, familiar places echo with memory and incident. The fingerprint of the past is everywhere: See the stair treads smoothed by countless feet, and the height lines scored into the doorframe. Cocooning, drifting with ghosts, these homes are like cinema itself. With this eclectic collection, we gather around the hearth for stories about home in all its manifestations and potential for revelation. Come in—it’s an open house!

title: A Tender Age:
subtitle: Two by Akinola Davies Jr.
full_title: A Tender Age:  Two by Akinola Davies Jr.
short_synopsis: Akinola Davies Jr. writes his acclaimed coming-of-age films with his brother, resurfacing shared memories of what it is to look at the world with uncorrupted eyes. Set in a changing Lagos, each tale entwines Nigeria’s histories with the smaller, but still profound, discoveries of growing up.
long_synopsis: “Change is coming,” anticipates one transitory character in My Father’s Shadow (2025). He is speculating from Lagos streets in 1993, where the hope of new democratic governance suffuses the summer air; an election is on the horizon, and Nigeria will indeed be changed come fall. But Akinola Davies Jr., the writer-director behind this acclaimed debut feature, situates his film less with the veteran outlook of voting-age adults than with two innocent siblings. One is eleven, the other is eight, and their understanding of Nigeria matures alongside more intimate realizations: tactile, personal, and diminutive, though no less profound for it. Davies Jr. wrote this and an earlier short, Lizard (2020), with his brother, drawing on their shared memories of childhood in Lagos to recall what it is like to look at the world with uncorrupted eyes: intuitively, curiously, sometimes mischievously. (“I just tasted beer,” one precocious adventurer grins.) Like the boys in My Father’s Shadow—with their quicksilver, excitable attention—the watchful girl in Lizard is attuned to the ground-level realm of little creatures: that reptile of the title, and other critters besides. In each film, use of 16mm adds texture to tortoise shells and ant carapaces, to the feathers of chicks, as the mobile camera hovers near to the children, discovering along with them. Looking up, there are skies, minarets, and adults’ bodies—formidable and vulnerable with secrets. Often, Davies Jr.‘s protagonists wander to corridors and backrooms where they spy flirtation for which they don’t yet have the words. They similarly encounter, at too tender an age, violence. Yet few can choose when the pains of growing up begin; while premonitions warn against coming danger, nothing inevitable can be warded off.

title: My Undesirable Friends:
subtitle: Part I — Last Air in Moscow
full_title: My Undesirable Friends: Part I — Last Air in Moscow
short_synopsis: Across five potent, immersive chapters, this vital work of long-form filmmaking accompanies young women journalists through the terror and absurdity of state persecution. Critically acclaimed, Julia Loktev’s epic captures the integrity and courage of political resistance with unparalleled intimacy.
long_synopsis: Across five potent, immersive chapters, this vital work of long-form filmmaking accompanies young women journalists through the terror and absurdity of state persecution. Critically acclaimed, Julia Loktev’s epic captures the integrity and courage of political resistance with unparalleled intimacy.

title: Windows to the Soul:
subtitle: Three by Jonathan Demme
full_title: Windows to the Soul:  Three by Jonathan Demme
short_synopsis: A jack-of-all-trades who mastered them too, Jonathan Demme could seemingly do anything. The films of this great American humanist are united by a limitless curiosity about the world around them. The beloved director’s legendary compassion radiates from every frame in these artful yet poppy classics.
long_synopsis: A face, center screen. Two eyes gaze intently down the barrel of the lens. Offering its own frank stare in return, the camera scours every flicker of emotion, opening a window to the soul. Jonathan Demme’s legendary close-ups are the mainstay of a shape-shifting filmography. A jack-of-all-trades who mastered them too, Demme was equally at home making screwball comedies, mafia flicks, and procedural horrors. Radiating compassion and good humor from every frame, this disparate body of work is united by its seemingly limitless curiosity. This American humanist wanted to understand everyone and everything. Even when confronting humanity’s darker instincts, his cinema is filled with the brightness of life; love, laughter, and music, especially, are never far away. No wonder his poppy yet artful films are so beloved by audiences and filmmakers alike. When asked to name his three favorite directors, Paul Thomas Anderson once answered: “Jonathan Demme, Jonathan Demme, and Jonathan Demme.” This collection showcases the range of a Swiss Army director with three electric classics. Leaving dull suburbia in the rearview mirror, Demme’s lust for life is the antidote to yuppie conservatism in the irrepressibly energetic Something Wild  (1986). Letting air and sunlight into the smoke-filled back rooms of the mafia movie, meanwhile, the sweetly romantic Married to the Mob (1988) flaunts the director’s love of actors with its inspired ensemble. One of only three films to win in all “Big Five” categories at the Oscars®, The Silence of the Lambs (1991) pits Demme’s legendary compassion against the depths of human evil. But Demme knows that life shines through if you know where to look—it may be as simple as a woman belting Tom Petty down a night-cloaked highway.

title: Kleber Mendonça Filho:
subtitle: Tropical Anxieties
short_synopsis: With his Oscar®-nominated The Secret Agent arriving in cinemas, this collection traces the evolution of Kleber Mendonça Filho’s singular style, from his early shorts to recent acclaimed features. Charting a changing Brazil, these eclectic marvels tackle societal upheaval with mischievous absurdism.
long_synopsis: One of Brazil’s most exciting contemporary voices, Kleber Mendonça Filho is back with The Secret Agent (2025), an Oscar®-nominated marvel now arriving in cinemas. The director’s hometown of Recife, an enduring source of inspiration, provides the setting for a number of early shorts, including Green Vinyl (2005) and Eletrodoméstica (2005). Shifting between genres—from modern-day folktales to sci-fi parables—these films witness the changes that swept through the northeastern port city with the coming of the new millennium. The tension in these works between past and present technologies manifests in Mendonça Filho’s eclectic visual style, which experiments with celluloid film stock, still photography, and lo-fi digital video. In both his fiction and documentary works, Mendonça Filho pays careful attention to architecture, eschewing empty nostalgia to lay bare complex financial and political forces behind city planning. While Neighbouring Sounds (2012) orchestrates a symphony of urban anxiety out of construction noises, Pictures of Ghosts (2023) traces Recife’s history of gentrification through its vanishing cinema palaces. Tackling societal upheaval with mischievous absurdism, these are boundary-pushing works from a chameleonic talent.

title: Double Act:
subtitle: Paolo Sorrentino and Toni Servillo
full_title: Double Act:  Paolo Sorrentino and Toni Servillo
short_synopsis: Director Paolo Sorrentino and actor Toni Servillo have worked side-by-side for fifteen years, shaping filmmaking culture far beyond their native Italy. Pairing an early critical success with their first international triumph, we celebrate the range and creativity of their vital collaboration.
long_synopsis: Director Paolo Sorrentino and actor Toni Servillo have worked side-by-side for fifteen years: a colorful collaboration that has influenced filmmaking far beyond their native Italy. To mark the theatrical release of La Grazia (2025), we chart a course from an early critical success to the film that launched their international careers. Servillo brings a compact charisma to The Great Beauty (2013), a serpentine tour of a debauched and devout Rome. His character looks neat as a pin in suits of cool blue, Tricolore red, and so comfortable in his skin that he looks at home anywhere—conga line or basilica. But while his Jep may seem a round peg in a round hole, this onetime novelist turned cultural critic only watches and contemplates, never really takes part. In The Great Beauty, Sorrentino has an actor able to telegraph—with depressed eyebrow, fluttering upper lip—an interiority rocked by the secondary waves of some historical wake. What may pass for torpor in Sorrentino’s second feature, The Consequences of Love (2004), is actually a state of preparedness. Servillo’s chain-smoking, poker-faced Titta is not only a coiled spring, but a creature of habit: Every day for eight years, he has stayed in the same hotel and sits in the same chair in the lobby bar. To play so placid a character and still fascinate is a talent few actors possess. Servillo has that talent, and it acts like gravity on this riveting film about a life held in abeyance, then exploding into change. From the touch-paper potential of the earlier film to the Felliniesque sweep of the later, this double bill showcases the extraordinary breadth of a still flourishing partnership.

title: Outta the Way!:
subtitle: Women at Play
full_title: Outta the Way!:  Women at Play
short_synopsis: Name-calling, brat energy, clowning around: There are 101 ways to stick it to the man when you’re a woman—and have a whole lot of fun while you’re at it! Transgressions big and small animate these playful films, which celebrate feminist rebellion and refusal this Women’s History Month.
long_synopsis: Name-calling, knowing gaucherie, choosing pleasure over compliance: There are 101 ways to stick it to the man when you’re a woman. For Women’s History Month, this riotous collection surveys the manifold inventive ways that feminist rebellion and refusal can surface. Transgressions of every scale animate these films, from fountain-like displays of wilful destruction to quietly finagled escapes from gender roles. No pious evangelizing, here: These films skew playful, sometimes absurdist, their heroines showing contempt for propriety and the notionally impermissible with brio, style, and maximal drama. Self-enrolled in a program of out-and-out pugnacity, some characters in this collection set out to create chaos. (Why step away from broken crockery when the shards might be ground under stilettoed foot?) Others act on a sudden impulse to throw out social niceties, surprising even themselves as they cause a stir. In whatever spirit they get started—and at whatever age—there’s no stopping these characters once they make a break for it. Teaming outstanding women directors with male directors who can be counted as allies, this errant collection vaults those social barriers and runs rings around the patriarchy!

title: Frederick Wiseman:
subtitle: American Lives
short_synopsis: The documentary filmmaker Frederick Wiseman, who died in February 2026, didn’t confine his filmmaking to the United States, but his work will forever be synonymous with an expansive anatomization of American institutions. This panoramic collection surveys the inestimable legacy of a cinematic titan.
long_synopsis: When Frederick Wiseman died in February 2026, American cinema lost one of its titans. For almost 60 years, the documentary filmmaker had traveled light, packing a microphone and sound recorder that he himself would wield, while his trusted cinematographer brandished a lightweight camera. It was from such modest means that Wiseman actualized galaxies: vast assemblages of unassuming moments that would constellate into a collective splendor. Wiseman’s observational style was much imitated, but never bettered. He would bristle at well-meaning accusations of objectivity, citing the “millions of choices” he would make in the editing room. Wiseman never editorialized though narration or direct-to-camera address, but his disposition—his politics, his point of view—underlies every frame of his 47 features. Wiseman didn’t confine his filmmaking to the United States, but his work will forever be synonymous with an expansive anatomization of American institutions. Whether in service of work, study, or play, these sophisticated machines are stripped for their constituent parts under Wiseman’s watchful gaze; every nut and bolt inspected in a bid to discover how the engine runs. “I’ve been involved in a 50-year course in adult education, where I’m the alleged adult who studies a new subject every year,” said Wiseman when he collected his honorary Oscar® in 2016. While the subject matter of each film changes, Wiseman’s fascination with the nuances of human behavior charts steady course. His is an unfailingly democratic anthropology—the caretaker as important a subject as the head of the board. This panoramic collection surveys the inestimable legacy of a filmmaker who generously surveyed the American Century with inexhaustible curiosity.

title: In the Realm of Melodrama:
subtitle: Two by Douglas Sirk
full_title: In the Realm of Melodrama: Two by Douglas Sirk
short_synopsis: Douglas Sirk’s opulent Technicolor melodramas weaponize sensation to mount sophisticated critiques of social convention, exploding the distance between art and trash. Traversing the known universe of human emotion, the master of irony discovers the meaning of happiness in the wilds of heartache.
long_synopsis: It is hard to believe that there was a time in which the films of German exile Douglas Sirk were disparaged as “weepies” or “soap operas.” Sirk’s 1950s melodramas, with their vivid use of Technicolor, blitzkriegs of emotion, and cannily deployed stars, weaponize sensation to mount sophisticated critiques of social conventions. “There is a very short distance between high art and trash,” the director once said. Now revered as jewels in the crown of Hollywood’s golden age, Sirk’s films explode that distance with ironic panache. Rock Hudson radiates brightest starlight in the first of his iconic collaborations with Sirk, keeping pace with the director’s most baroque twists in Magnificent Obsession (1954). Collaborating once again, the two decamped to the drill-ravaged deserts of American petrocapitalism for Sirk’s extravagantly Gothic memento mori, Written On The Wind (1956). Traversing the known universe of human emotion, Sirk discovers the meaning of happiness in the harshest wilds of heartache.

title: Udo Kier:
subtitle: Scene Stealer
short_synopsis: With his piercing gaze and sonorous voice, Udo Kier was an indelible presence onscreen, lending gravitas and an edge of danger to even the briefest of performances. Following the devastating news of his death, this collection pays tribute to a film icon for whom there are truly no small parts.
long_synopsis: With his piercing gaze and sonorous voice, Udo Kier is an indelible presence onscreen. In a legendary career that stretches over 50 years and nearly 300 acting credits, the film icon has played seemingly every imaginable archetype, from diabolical doctors and campy hairdressers to vampires and aliens from outer space. As his Adonis good looks matured, Kier transitioned into character roles with aplomb, channeling a sense of gravitas and an edge of danger into even the briefest of performances. His predilection for the strange and otherworldly has led to memorable collaborations with like-minded auteurs on boundary-pushing works. Following the devastating news of his death, this special collection pays tribute to a consummate actor for whom there are truly no small parts.

title: Heart Before Head:
subtitle: The Rom-Com Variations
full_title: Heart Before Head: The Rom-Com Variations
short_synopsis: Look away from the classics, and alternative rom-coms appear across cinema history, hidden there like little loved-up secrets. Variations on cherished formulas, these surprise rom-coms might take different paths to romance, but are irresistible, and deserve our affections too.
long_synopsis: Some rom-coms we know by heart. They start with a meet-cute on the street corner, in the school hall, in the office elevator. Then the flirtatious equivocations begin: Will they or won’t they? Finally, the smitten couple embrace—before fireworks, let’s say, that light up the night skies. That reliable formula for catching butterflies is cherished in this swooning collection—but this time, we’re widening the net on what gets called “rom-com,” and falling for the unexpected. Look away from the genre’s classics—’30s screwballs, ’50s sex farces, and confections from the ’90s—and alternative rom-coms appear across cinema history, hidden there like loved-up secrets. So the path to true romance is different here. Who cares at all, when sparks are so obviously flying! If we put heart before head, then we have, eventually, to admit it: Little variations on the rom-com like this are irresistible, and deserve our affections too.

title: “Big Five”
subtitle: Oscar Winners
short_synopsis: Some Academy Awards® may be said to sparkle more than others. For those lucky few who win the prize for best picture, director, actress, actor, or screenplay, it is a “Big Five” Oscar®—and this luminous collection celebrates those superlative films that succeeded in going for top-tier gold.
long_synopsis: Since the inception of the Academy Awards® in the late 1920s, various talents have been celebrated with its gleaming statuettes. Yet a choice handful of trophies, whose prestige goes back to the very first ceremony, may be said to sparkle more than some others. Those lucky few who come away from Hollywood’s main event with the prize for best picture, director, actress, actor, or screenplay have won one of the fabled “Big Five,” often counting among the most iconic features in Oscar® history. This luminous collection centers films that took at least one “Big Five” Oscar®—although some, like Norman Jewison’s In the Heat of the Night (1967), Jonathan Demme’s The Silence of the Lambs (1991), and Bong Joon Ho’s Parasite (2019), walked off with three or more. Roses are thrown in admiration of riveting scripts, towering performances and unparalleled filmmaking visions, while we look back at classics that succeeded in going for top-tier gold.

title: Crash, Bang, Wallop:
subtitle: The Cinema of Buster Keaton
full_title: Crash, Bang, Wallop: The Cinema of Buster Keaton
short_synopsis: The most modern of the silent clowns, Buster Keaton expanded the possibilities of filmmaking both behind the camera and in front of it. Endlessly innovative, miraculously athletic, and sublimely silly, Keaton pursued laughter at all costs—and elevated an art form in the process.
long_synopsis: Buster Keaton rewrote the rules of cinema and induced new kinds of laughter. The silent comic’s technical audacity was as breathtaking as his hilarity, as in the Sherlock Jr. (1924) sequence when he clambers inside a film projection. The General (1926), meanwhile, contains some of the most implausible stunts recorded on film, including the spectacle of an entire steam train plunging from a bridge. In Buster’s world, playfulness, more than necessity, is the mother of invention. Keaton’s most effective trick is his most disarmingly simple: the canny placement of the human figure in space. His pliant form was a special effect like no other. During staggering chase sequences in Our Hospitality (1923) and Seven Chances (1925), we see Buster casually dust himself off after violating fundamental laws of physics. More than technical wizardry, this is the visionary essence of Keaton’s cinema: a pure play between camera, body, and world.

title: Next Episode:
subtitle: Series
short_synopsis: The series is a generous form, inspiring filmmakers to greater heights of ambition and invention. Whether immersing audiences in extensive, densely populated worlds, or forgoing narrative in favor of something nearer collage, these series have us hooked, and powerless to stop watching. Next episode!
long_synopsis: The series is a generous form, broadening the possibilities for visual storytelling. Opening the door to bigger and bolder filmmaking, the series accommodates vastness and complexity: more, more, and still more! Historically, it has proven well-suited to sweeping narratives that traverse borders and sagas that stretch across time periods, excavating origin stories or gathering up generations of a family. Long-form storytelling also allows for engaging adaptations, which transfer novelistic texture and detail from page to screen. With the additional space and time that multiple episodes afford, filmmakers can play generatively and subversively with genre conventions, explore parallel realities with extensive world-building, or—mixing registers like paints—fashion tonally eccentric works consistent with a singular vision. Some series eschew narrative altogether, but are no less immersive for that. Their makers are drawn to the patchwork and collaging potential of episodes, emphasising not sameness and connection, but difference and disjunction. However it fits together, a series bids us to get involved, to puzzle over the pieces of the story. Featuring ensemble casts filled invariably with your favorite character actors, series draw us deeper and deeper in, and hold us there. It’s hopeless: The series in this collection are so engrossing, we can’t resist the invitation to keep watching. Next episode, please.

title: Shooting Stars:
subtitle: Hollywood's Next Legends
short_synopsis: While young stars move at lightning speed from one project to the next, there is pleasure in revisiting those early roles that saw their careers arc off the chart. This twinkling collection constellates today’s most talked-about actors and celebrates their turning-point performances.
long_synopsis: One film is all it takes. A single performance can send an actor’s career into the stratosphere. And from there, there’s no looking back. Inundated with offers, their schedules filling up, these actors haven’t time to dwell on past achievements. Their audiences and fans, however, can take enormous pleasure in revisiting their radiant beginnings. As they arc across the sky at lightning speed, these shooting stars carry with them something of those early turns that introduced them to the world. Inside their later performances nests some vestige of the role that changed everything. Though they go on to greater and bigger things, like summer blockbusters and Oscar® wins, something of that first impression lasts and becomes part of their aura. It might be a particular character that sticks, or something smaller: some memorable gesture that sets us laughing, the wounded look that lets us in, or the twitching facial muscle that gives away the lie. Hollywood careers are built on the molecular details of performance: the little miracle revelations of an actor’s originality. But such gestural ingenuities make no impact at all if they do not telegraph deep currents of feeling, or expose the shifting plates of a troubled interiority. Only the best young actors of a generation have it in them to carry the soul of a film, to spin a narrative on its axis with only a backward glance. This twinkling collection constellates today’s most talked-about stars and relives the excitement they generated on first appearance. These brilliant films catch young performers on the upward swing: safely out of obscurity, squinting in the glare of the limelight, and uncertain of what the future holds. Now they know. The stars in this collection have won critical acclaim and the affections of global audiences, who recognize the making of a Hollywood giant in each meteoric trajectory.

title: Nobody's Perfect:
subtitle: Three Comedies by Billy Wilder
full_title: Nobody's Perfect: Three Comedies by Billy Wilder
short_synopsis: Billy Wilder and I. A. L. Diamond are one of the greatest writer-director duos of Hollywood’s golden age. Bringing together three of their most beloved comedies, this collection honors a creative partnership that celebrated the frustrations and contradictions of our shared humanity.
long_synopsis: If we were to select a single frame of film to represent the cinema of Billy Wilder, we might well turn to a shot from his 1960 Best Picture–winner, The Apartment. It comes at the end of the first act, with Jack Lemmon’s perpetually sniffling office drone pacing forlornly outside a theater in the cold, stood up by his date under a sign advertising “one of the best musical-comedies of our time.” The Apartment may not have any musical numbers, but the marquee—juxtaposed with Lemmon’s pitiful despondency—embodies the contradictions in the Wilder worldview: that even joy and laughter are invariably flavored with a tincture of melancholy and woe. It’s tempting to lay such dualities at the feet of collaboration. The screenwriter I. A. L. Diamond first joined forces with Wilder in 1957, cementing their position among the greatest writers of comic dialogue across seven films. Diamond was the ideal foil to the gloominess of Wilder’s outlook, which had previously colored his pungent noirs and coruscating satires in varying shades of tar. Led by Diamond’s exuberant pen, Wilder gave himself over to ebullience with Some Like It Hot (1959), The Apartment, and Irma La Douce (1963), inviting lightness and bounce into what are all essentially sad tales of lonely souls afraid to connect outside the amoral strictures of their work environment. In putting their duelling creative sensibilities up on the screen, these temperamental opposites were able to capture the fundamental turmoil of their characters’ humanity. “You need to be a mensch!” says Jack Kruschen’s neighboring doctor to a floundering Lemmon in The Apartment. “You know what that means? A mensch? A human being!” On the strength of the films in this collection, one gets the impression that Wilder and Diamond understand him implicitly.

title: Cinema by Any Means:
subtitle: A Jafar Panahi Retrospective
full_title: Cinema by Any Means:  A Jafar Panahi Retrospective
short_synopsis: Dedicated to the irrepressible auteur Jafar Panahi, this retrospective celebrates a towering figure of artistic resilience. There is no limit that the Iranian filmmaker won’t overcome: In the face of censorship, he continues to make poetic, independent cinema by any means possible.
long_synopsis: An international symbol of artistic resilience and defiance, Iranian auteur Jafar Panahi imbues his works with an intense empathy for those living under an oppressive regime. For this, he has been arrested, imprisoned, and banned from making films by Iranian authorities. Coinciding with the release of his new film, It Was Just An Accident (2025), a Palme d’Or–winning and Oscar®-nominated marvel, this extensive retrospective celebrates an irrepressible force in contemporary cinema. Written by Abbas Kiarostami, Panahi’s debut, The White Balloon (1995), exemplifies his humanistic spirit. Along with The Mirror (1997), the two films take a child’s-eye view of bustling Tehran, turning ordinary tasks into urban adventures. Both banned in his home country, The Circle (2000) and Offside (2006) deepen Panahi’s inquiry into the sexist treatment of women and girls in Iran, for whom barriers exist not only in prison, but in all facets of life. As well as using realism to confront urgent issues, Panahi’s films give rein to witty experimentation. While Crimson Gold (2003) uses the crime genre to comment on wealth inequality, Closed Curtain (2013) turns Panahi’s own house arrest into a moving meditation on injustice and creativity. It’s one of the director’s many films that blend documentary and fiction, and one of the many in which he appears as himself. In 3 Faces (2018), he steps behind the wheel, drives around Iran, and interacts with his compatriots. Here as elsewhere, cinema emerges as a potent tool of resistance, even in times of fear. There is no limit that Panahi won’t overcome: No matter the obstacle, he goes on to make his thoroughly independent cinema by any means possible.

title: Between Tides:
subtitle: Asian Avant-garde
short_synopsis: Presented by MUBI in partnership with CHANEL Culture Fund and M+ Museum, this collection celebrates the bold voices of Asian avant-garde cinema through experimental works that reflect on the past and imagine new futures.
long_synopsis: Between Tides: Asian Avant-garde, presented by MUBI in partnership with CHANEL Culture Fund and M+ Museum, traces the bold, shifting contours of contemporary Asian cinema. This collection gathers filmmakers from across the region whose visions move beyond convention—ranging from poetic shorts to boundary-pushing features, fiction to experimental documentary. Earlier works by pioneering auteurs challenge boundaries and advocate for alternative expression, while recent films turn to the ecological, unearthing hidden histories and memories bound to landscape. Amid lingering complexities, many of these films find freedom in imagining the past or casting light on contemporary realities. Cross-cultural and interdisciplinary, each work pushes cinema’s limits—reflecting on what was, and what is yet to come.

title: Should Old Acquaintance Be Forgot?:
subtitle: New Year's on Film
full_title: Should Old Acquaintance Be Forgot?: New Year's on Film
short_synopsis: One of the most visually splendorous and emotionally poignant days of the year, New Year’s Eve offers filmmakers spectacular opportunities. Its sparkling and colorful festivities light up the frame, and make for intensely felt works of beginnings and endings, filled with the stuff of life.
long_synopsis: One of the most visually splendorous days of the year, New Year’s Eve offers filmmakers spectacular opportunities. Its festivities light up the frame: Parties glow with jubilant decorations; colorful fireworks bloom against the black midwinter sky. Positioned at the changing of the calendar, the final day of the year is also enticingly dramatic. In these intensely felt works, old relationships are revisited and new ones start with a midnight kiss; amends are made for the past and resolutions set for the future. A moment for revelry and hope, New Year’s marks the passing of time and carries with it notes of poignancy. Looking backward as well as forward, the bustling titles in this collection are filled with crowds gathered for remembrance, celebration, and merry-making—in short, the stuff of life.

title: Double Act:
subtitle: Emma Seligman and Rachel Sennott
full_title: Double Act:  Emma Seligman and Rachel Sennott
short_synopsis: The formidable cowriting partnership of director Emma Seligman and actor Rachel Sennott has changed the face of coming-of-age comedy. Jabbing the zeitgeist in the eye, these feisty films riff mischievously on the awkwardness of sexual adventure and self-expression in the age of digital nativism.
long_synopsis: In a few short years, writer-director Emma Seligman and actor of the moment Rachel Sennott have together turned the coming-of-age genre inside out and back to front. Born in 1995—which makes them neither millennials nor Zoomers, but a self-conscious cocktail of both—these young collaborators have made a study of female sexuality’s new realities. A shared sense of humor, and ambition belying their years, underpins their different but complementary gifts. Sennott was clueless, she claims, about narrative structure and character arc before meeting Seligman, whose singular vision and discipline impressed her. Sennott’s comedic improvisation and her fearlessness before a camera, meanwhile, lift their wired, self-satirizing dialogue off the page. The pair met at New York University when Sennott auditioned for Seligman’s thesis film, a short that would form the basis of Seligman’s debut feature, Shiva Baby (2020). Loading gaucherie on ungainliness on gaffe like so much lox on a bagel, this perversely subversive drama was received by audiences as a ready-made cult classic, enjoying an unprecedented sixteen-week run at New York’s Quad Cinema. A disarmingly raw performer, Sennott shines as a chaotic college-age naïf forced to appraise her life—sex, friendship, faith, the whole schmear—over the course of an afternoon. Sennott stepped into the role of cowriter for their next collaboration. In coffee shops across New York, the pair chipped away at a screenplay, with Seligman typing at her laptop and Sennott rising from her chair to act out scenes and dry-run dialogue. Queering the high-school film and dragging it by the braids into the 2020s, Bottoms (2023) blends Gen Alpha nihilism, pornsickness, and obsessional lust into a chuggable protein shake of a teen comedy. Looking the zeitgeist right in the eye and detaching its retina with a jab, these feisty films tackle outmoded gender archetypes to the gym floor, as they introduce new sex-positive paradigms for the age of digital nativism.

title: Truly, Madly, Deeply
short_synopsis: Romance can be uncomplicated for some: fall in love, walk into the sunset, and live happily ever after. That, however, is not how it unfolds in these intensely amorous films. Truly, madly, deeply desirous, their fervent characters turn the world upside down, head over heels and addicted to love.
long_synopsis: Romance can be uncomplicated for some: fall in love, walk into the sunset, and live happily ever after. That, however, is not how it unfolds in this amorous collection. Seeing an ill-fated tinderbox in that dollhouse scenario, these incendiary films strike matches against narratives of cozy contentment. Living in married unison is not the blissful state of affairs we often dream. In uncontrollable flames, love smolders with wild passion, sparking chaos and conflict too. Sweethearts darken with sudden obsession, and couples tumultuously yo-yo in and out of affection: breaking up, then making up, then coming undone again. Truly, madly, deeply desirous, the hot-blooded characters in these fervent films turn the world upside down, head over heels and addicted to love.

title: Trans Awareness Week:
subtitle: Four Essentials of Trans Cinema
full_title: Trans Awareness Week: Four Essentials of Trans Cinema
short_synopsis: These essentials of trans cinema examine the multitude of experiences and identities within the trans community with dignity and playfulness. Recording the daily realities of the trans experience or rehearsing a better world to come, these stories of endurance and becoming glimpse liberation.
long_synopsis: “Every individual life is a collective history,” narrates Paul B. Preciado in Orlando, My Political Biography (2023), a generous examination of the myriad experiences and identities within the trans community. Riffing expansively on Virginia Woolf’s seminal novel, Orlando (1928), Preciado’s celebratory documentary insists that the story of a community cannot be told without telling the particular stories of those within it. The films in this collection prove that cinema can be an essential means of representing those lives. Drawing attention to the issues raised by Trans Awareness Week, this collection places influential representations of trans lives alongside more recent explorations of the contemporary trans experience. Offering resolute perspectives on the social realities of marginalization and violence, Pedro Almodóvar’s groundbreaking All About Your Mother (1999) and Andrea Pallaoro’s dignified Monica (2022) are, among other things, indispensable records of the injustices faced by a community. The trans experience is defined by more than just struggle. With flair and an aptitude for mischief, the filmmakers and performers in this collection celebrate the playful, transformative possibilities that emerge when simple binaries are refused in life and art. As one performer in Jennie Livingston’s iconic time capsule of the New York ballroom scene, Paris Is Burning (1990), tells us: “In a ballroom, you can be anything you want.” Documenting the daily realities of the trans experience, or rehearsing a more humane world to come, these stories of endurance and becoming glimpse liberation at 24 frames per second.

title: Dream Factory:
subtitle: Los Angeles on Screen
full_title: Dream Factory:  Los Angeles on Screen
short_synopsis: Hope and cynicism, illusion and reality, pornography and art: Los Angeles is a fascinating tangle of contradictions. Whether following the fame-hungry into chaos or tracing the heart lines of lovers, these films take us up into the hills and under the silver lake in hot pursuit of dreams.
long_synopsis: Dreams are made in Los Angeles. The gleaming Hollywood sign smiles on the upbeat and ambitious, who come to the entertainment capital of the world seeking fame and fortune. But dreams also spoil in this fickle city. The luster doesn’t last. It flakes away in Renaissance-style mansions. Lucky breaks turn up face-down in the pool. And don’t be fooled by that beaming landmark drilled into the hillside: The sign is repainted once a decade in a custom-made shade of perfect white. Hope and cynicism, hyperbole and reality, pornography and art: Los Angeles is a tangle of contradictions, yanked tight. Generations of fascinated filmmakers have worked away at that knot, teasing apart the real and the illusory, or—like the late, great David Lynch—playing into the paradox. From Mulholland Drive to Skid Row, this open-top tour of a collection explores all corners of the city—the tinselled and the tucked away. Mapping the snakes-and-ladders progression of wannabes and has-beens, or tracing a finger along the heart lines of ordinary Angelenos, these films stake out a range of experiences in a wide array of visual styles. In the words of urban theorist Mike Davis: “Sunshine or Noir?” These are the axes of Los Angeles, a place that inspires fleet-footed romances and eerie anatomies of its shadowy perversity.

title: Maiden Voyages:
subtitle: 2025's Outstanding Debut Women Directors
full_title: Maiden Voyages:  2025's Outstanding Debut Women Directors
short_synopsis: It’s harvest time, and we’re celebrating the year’s bumper crop of debut features by women and non-binary directors. Whether crossing over from acting or screenwriting, or emerging as an auteur from the off, these filmmakers have hit the ground running with films deserving of first-place rosettes.
long_synopsis: It’s harvest season, and 2025 has seen a bumper crop of debut features by women and non-binary directors. Whether making the leap from acting or screenwriting, or emerging as an auteur from the off, these still-green filmmakers have hit the ground running with films deserving of first-place rosettes. Sorry, Baby (2025) is the newest of the six debuts in this collection, impressing with delicate humor about a difficult subject matter. Comedian Eva Victor explores the fine line between therapeutic solitude and out-and-out loneliness in this tender tale of life after trauma. Directing themselves in the lead role, and harnessing their trademark feel for the absurd, Victor allows their protagonist to mend at their own sporadic pace, with bursts of self-assertion that extend to spontaneous sex with a neighbor. The “here” of home is central to the poetic Tendaberry (2024), its director Haley Elizabeth Anderson fixing South Brooklyn with a gimlet eye. In open traffic with the past, her corner of New York feels joined-up and lived-in: worn-in and comfortable like a favorite jacket. Into its lining, Anderson sews a more traditional Bildungsroman narrative: a year in the life of a spirited twenty-something called Dakota. The younger characters of Toxic (2024) and September Says (2024) experience something close to motion sickness, as they pitch and toss in the undulating waltzer ride of adolescence. In Saulė Bliuvaitė’s unblinking film, just-teenage children are abandoned to boundary-less play and experimentation in Lithuania’s edgelands. A Locarno prizewinner, Toxic insists that meaningful connections transcend the most hostile of environments. The inverse is true in Ariane Labed’s imaginative debut: Where the sisters of September Says once sought refuge, they come to harm in each other’s arms. India Donaldson’s absorbing debut Good One (2024) and Rebecca Lenkiewicz’s arresting Hot Milk (2025) explore the parent–child relationship by relocating it to new terrains. Donaldson’s queer protagonist Sam has the acceptance of her loving father. But a back-to-nature expedition sends these campers back in time, turning up outworn gender norms that send Sam’s compass spinning. Good One’s verdant greens contrast with the clay and mineral tones of Hot Milk, which fires the mother–daughter dynamic in the kiln of desert Spain. There, Lenkiewicz’s heroine Sofia chooses sensuous, stinging life over the deceptive safety of codependence. These maiden voyages by women and non-binary directors call for celebration. We’re breaking a bottle of champagne against their hulls, and anticipate with excitement the careers that lie ahead.

title: Ban This Sick Filth!:
subtitle: Video Nasties
full_title: Ban This Sick Filth!: Video Nasties
short_synopsis: In the summer of 1982, Britain’s high streets were engulfed in moral panic. “Ban video sadism now,” roared one tabloid headline. “We must protect our children,” cried another. This collection brings together some highlights from the infamous Video Nasty list, presented uncut in all their gory glory.
long_synopsis: “Ban video sadism now,” roared one all-caps headline of the Daily Mail. “Burn your video nasty,” echoed The Sun. In the summer of 1982, Britain was engulfed in moral panic, the ire of the tabloids trained on the high-street video stores that had quickly become public enemy number one. As populist guardians of public morality, egged on by the Christian activist and all-round busybody Mary Whitehouse, sections of the conservative press were outraged by the lurid art that adorned VHS cases across the nation. The content within, which they invariably hadn’t seen, was beside the point. Here was a clear-cut story of good versus evil: the unregulated video market cast in the role of villain; Britain’s poor children—exposed to “the worst excesses of cinema sex and violence”—their scarred and dangerously perturbed victims. It sure made for great copy. Parliament was spurred into action, and in 1984, the Video Recordings Act was passed, making it a criminal offence to rent or sell any title that hadn’t been approved by the British Board of Film Censors. A naughty list of 72 titles was initially drawn up by the Director of Public Prosecutions, consigning the likes of Lucio Fulci’s House by the Cemetery (1981) and Abel Ferrara’s The Driller Killer (1979) to immediate infamy as “Video Nasties.” Other films, like George A. Romero’s Night of the Living Dead (1968) and Dario Argento’s Deep Red (1975), while not deemed “obscene” like those on the Nasty list, were also subject to seizure. Some 40 years later, almost all of the original Video Nasties have been passed uncut for UK distribution, with the BBFC having rebranded itself as a body for classification rather than censorship. This collection brings together some highlights from the original lists, presented in all their gory glory. If the extremity herein gets a bit much, try taking some advice from an iconic Video Nasty poster: “To avoid fainting / keep repeating / it’s only a movie… / it’s only a movie… / it’s only a movie.”

title: Back in the Game
short_synopsis: Few things unite the entertainment press like an actor’s return to play. Showcasing three revelatory performances from Demi Moore, Mickey Rourke, and Pamela Anderson, this collection applauds the late-career turns and fairy-tale transformations that ushered in new and exciting artistic directions.
long_synopsis: Few things unite the entertainment press like an actor’s return to the spotlight. Cover shoots, celebrity profiles, and red-carpet coverage restore these players to the center of attention, but only on condition that they snap back better than ever. In their prime and back in the limelight, these stars redraw the arcs of their careers and have more than a sporting chance at awards success. By his own admission, life as an A-lister in 1980s Hollywood had Mickey Rourke on the ropes. Rumble Fish (1983) set in motion a six-year streak for the New York-born actor as a leading man, but he tapped out at the turn of the decade, defeated and out of favor. A faltering return to film in the 2000s turned into a textbook rebound when Darren Aronofsky made Rourke the main attraction of The Wrestler (2008). In this touching arthouse sports film, the onetime heartthrob played against type: Washed-up, swollen with steroids, and licking his wounds, his Randy “The Ram” Robinson is lovable, but no oil painting. A tearjerking turn in the high-grossing Ghost (1990) established Demi Moore as one of the most bankable stars of the 1990s. While no stranger to the erotic thriller, the legal drama, or romance, Moore played a danger hand with her starring role in The Substance (2024): a body-horror first for the actor. In this Cronenbergian cautionary tale for the era of total plasma exchange, Moore plays an aging star classed as a has-been by network executives. Whether outfacing her reflection in dialogue-less stalemate, or writhing in agonized panic in the white-tiled prison her bathroom becomes, Moore mesmerizes, and has the Golden Globe to prove it. Six feet tall in an ostrich-feather headdress, a vision in peach-melba hues, Pamela Anderson made an entrance and a striking return to cinema with The Last Showgirl (2024). Gia Coppola’s drama draws to an end the cynical stunt casting that trapped the actor in a cycle of cameos. Anderson enchants as a veteran dancer in the last Folies Bergère–style revue in Las Vegas, coming to terms with the show’s closure after 38 years on the Strip. Soft-focus handheld photography, magic-hour lens flares, and all the prismatic crystal detail of showgirl costuming make this the most romantic of reinventions. Career-best performances past the age of 50 shift the window of perspective on an actor’s body of work: Late-career highs neutralize talk of heydays and heights of fame, as a new story emerges. This special collection cheers three late-career performances, three thrilling returns to play, each one a dramatic transformation straight out of the fairy-tale treasury.

title: Performers We Love:
subtitle: Adam Driver
short_synopsis: A devoted craftsman, playful as he is serious, Adam Driver stands apart as one of the more daring and deliberate actors of his generation. As his upcoming films promise still more reinvention, this collection celebrates some of his most memorable performances and the ambition of his career to date.
long_synopsis: “I’m a difficult person. Everyone’s a difficult person.” Abrupt, unwashed, and honest to a fault, the character of Adam Sackler in Lena Dunham’s Girls (2012–17) was a knot of contradictions, one made plausible and (yes!) desirable by a breakout actor with the same first name. At 28, Adam Driver had an oil-and-water intrigue all of his own. He was a veteran of the Marine Corps and a graduate of The Juilliard School for performing arts; a broad-shouldered old soul in the mold of Robert Mitchum and a breath of fresh air all at once. Five years of profoundly imaginative character work on television were a superlative advertisement for Driver’s ability to embody complex characters, alchemizing “difficulty” into solid-gold charisma. First to make their approach were directors Noah Baumbach and Steven Spielberg. Then came the Coen Brothers, Jeff Nichols, and Jim Jarmusch. Martin Scorsese followed, and after him Steven Soderbergh, Spike Lee, and Terry Gilliam. Driver’s screen credits are a who’s who of contemporary cinema’s most accomplished directors; their disparate sensibilities speak volumes of Driver’s adaptability and the breadth of his appeal. This collection celebrates just that: the creativity of a protean actor whose stamina shows no signs of abating. Driver’s part in Frances Ha (2012), a privileged subletting millennial cosplaying as Jean-Paul Belmondo, marked the first of a five-film partnership with Baumbach. Still waters run deep with his versifying New Jersey bus driver in Paterson (2016), a meditation on the poetry of a modest life, lived moment to moment. From gentleness personified in standard-issue uniform to toxic masculinity in towelling dressing gown: In Leos Carax’s Annette (2021), Driver swaggers as a stand-up comedian, whose controversial sets are a warm-up for uxoricide. Pivoting again, Driver brings a low center of gravity to the architectural CG-spectacle of Megalopolis (2024), his soft-spoken engineer cantilevering Francis Ford Coppola’s $120 million–dollar dream palace. No two of his roles the same, Driver is one of the most versatile and daring actors of his generation. As his upcoming films promise still more reinvention, still more revelation, we revisit the turns that led him here.

title: People are Strange:
subtitle: Outsiders on Film
full_title: People are Strange:  Outsiders on Film
short_synopsis: Outlaws, eccentrics, and strays: Cinema throngs with unconventional characters who go their own way. This collection assembles films about the exploits and adventures of nonconformists. Free-spirited, vampire-bitten, or just plain strange, there’s company in the unconventional. Outsiders assemble!
long_synopsis: Cinema loves an outsider. From wuxia to film noir, creature features to westerns, unconventional characters are the meat and potatoes of entire genres of film, and the calling card of many a decorated director. The exploits and misadventures of the misfit take many forms: Women throwing off the yoke of domesticity; men with no names—cinema throngs with characters who buck the system and duck out of society. Driving into the sunset, they flip the bird to Big Brother. Then there are the fugitives, the vigilantes, and the searchers: spiritually homeless, and in dogged pursuit of retribution or peace of mind. In whatever guise it comes, outsider cinema trades on the irresistible appeal of nonconformity. At its best, it celebrates rebel sensibilities, life at the margins, and strength in eccentric numbers. This eclectic collection assembles some of the finest examples of that vibrant cinema. Whether shoaling with other fish out of water in the spirit of community, or embracing solitude and going it alone, rarities and originals are welcome here. Free-spirited, vampire-bitten, or just plain strange, there’s company in the unconventional. Go on: Fly your freak flag, and fly it high!

title: More More More:
subtitle: Films From the 80s
full_title: More More More: Films From the 80s
short_synopsis: In an era of rapid economic growth that promised opulence for all, the filmmakers of this collection reckoned with new definitions of happiness, dreams of flashy materialism, and careerist ambition. Made in the 1980s, these turbulent films are galvanized by the decade’s legendary exuberance.
long_synopsis: The 1980s were synonymous with excess. Working through those turbulent times, the films in this collection take the quickening pulse of a manic decade even as they hurl themselves into an uncertain future. In an era of rapid economic growth that promised opulence for all, filmmakers reckoned with new definitions of happiness: Their protagonists dream of climbing the corporate ladder, and hatch audacious get-rich-quick schemes to be able to indulge in flashy materialism. That restless hunger for affluence is palpable in the fast pace of many of these films, and in their intoxicating atmosphere, charged with desire. Subtlety was not the name of the game in 1980s cinema. Here, crowded frames and teeming interiors are oversaturated with stuff—the new décor of a proudly consumerist society. Filmmakers often deployed a maximalist aesthetic to match the ostentatious bling and bold, colorful fashions of their characters. But there wasn’t space at the top for everyone. Countering mainstream narratives of satisfaction, some directors turned their cameras to countercultural misfits who, wary of superficial beauty and conspicuous wealth, refused to conform to this brash modern world. Surfing the wave of change that defined the ‘80s, or holding strong against the current, the films in this collection are galvanized by the decade’s legendary exuberance.

title: Against the Grain:
subtitle: Two from Locarno
full_title: Against the Grain: Two from Locarno
short_synopsis: Committed to championing new voices, the Locarno Film Festival is home to the future of cinema. This collection brings together two award-winning titles from last year’s lineup—uncompromising tales of girlhood that fearlessly push against formal conventions and patriarchal dogmas.
long_synopsis: Committed to championing new voices, the Locarno Film Festival is home to the future of cinema. This collection brings together two critically acclaimed titles from last year’s lineup—uncompromising tales of girlhood that fearlessly push against formal conventions and patriarchal dogmas. Winner of the prestigious Golden Leopard, Saulė Bliuvaitė’s unflinching feature debut Toxic (2024) tempers the industrial landscape of Lithuania with youthful rebellion. Neglected at home and exploited by modelling agencies, two teenage girls find escape, not so much in elusive dreams of the fashion world, but through their unbreakable bond. While their bodies are subjected to relentless scrutiny, their spirits are movingly unbound, soaring above the desolate factories and sterile rehearsal rooms in which they spend their days. Awarded the Special Jury Prize, Kurdwin Ayub’s Moon (2024) similarly tackles the psychological and physical trappings of sexist environments. Following an Austrian martial artist hired to train three sisters in Jordan, this gripping multicultural thriller uncovers the dark turmoil that lies beneath the plush luxuries of an upper-class milieu, revealing a system of control and subjugation. Profoundly sympathetic toward the struggles of young girls trapped in impossible circumstances, these bracing works from rising women filmmakers boldly challenge the status quo.

title: Reunion Tour:
subtitle: Two by Alex Ross Perry
full_title: Reunion Tour: Two by Alex Ross Perry
short_synopsis: Alex Ross Perry is no people-pleaser, whether exposing egos through uniquely nervy character studies or experimenting with what the music documentary can be. In these two films about wayward performers, the independent filmmaker refuses to shut up and play the hits.
long_synopsis: In the 2010s, American writer-director Alex Ross Perry found critical acclaim with uniquely nervy character studies. Egos and neuroses were rife. The self-destructive punk rocker in Her Smell (2018) threatens spectacular breakdown, while alienating others with verbose arrogance and scathing wit. The ever-dynamic camera of cinematographer Sean Price Williams holds a cracked mirror to her erratic disposition—her screwed-up psyche glinting like shards of glass, and Elisabeth Moss giving a coruscating, dangerous performance to match. Pavements (2024) is Perry playing a new set of instruments. In this vertiginously hybrid documentary about an indie band from the ‘90s, a reunion tour is a catalyst for tributes that are, in many ways, anathema to what came before—for both Pavement and Perry. Embracing the heartfelt finesse of the jukebox musical and deriding the bland blueprint of the Hollywood biopic, the former sends Pavement’s lo-fi distortion and lackadaisical melodies soaring to enthusiastic heights, while the latter stands in extreme contrast to Perry’s independent attitude. It makes for a good, self-effacing joke, with a hilarious turn from Joe Keery. Whizzing together fake and real, ironic and sincere, Perry finds myriad ways to look at the past in this ambitious, dizzying film. For both band and filmmaker, it’s a brilliant comeback: one that refuses to shut up and play the hits.

title: Now in Glorious 4K!
short_synopsis: With crystal-clear images and immersive sound, these sublime 4K restorations reveal stunning detail in beloved works of cinema. Offering a fresh chance to marvel at revered classics and underseen gems, this collection holds riches for first-time and return viewers alike—and looks as good as new.
long_synopsis: With crystal-clear images and immersive sound, these sublime 4K restorations reveal stunning detail in beloved works of cinema. Offering a fresh chance to marvel at revered classics and underseen gems, this collection holds riches for first-time and return viewers alike—and looks as good as new.

title: Welcome to Twin Peaks
short_synopsis: All is not what it seems in this place “both wonderful and strange,” where the woods hold ancient secrets, and dreams bleed into the very fabric of reality. From the visionary minds of showrunners Mark Frost and David Lynch comes a TV phenomenon 30 years in the making. Welcome to Twin Peaks.
long_synopsis: The Douglas firs stand to attention on the long, meandering approach to the town of Twin Peaks, protectors of a closely guarded way of life. Time loses all meaning in this northwestern burg, “five miles south of the Canadian border, twelve miles west of the state line.” It’s a place where the fashions, hospitalities, and industries of the 1950s have no intention—some 40 years later—of giving up their claims on the local population’s cultural soul. The Double R Diner is the spot for “extraordinarily good coffee and pie,” and it’s easy to find “a clean place, reasonably priced,” in which to lay your head. A place is made by its people, and the inhabitants of Twin Peaks have stopped the clocks just the way they like it. Only a stranger, like the FBI agent marveling at the trees as he winds his way into town, would make a note of the time. But all is not what it seems in this place “both wonderful and strange.” The woods hold secrets of their own, dreams bleed into the very fabric of reality, and deeply buried horrors have a way of surfacing, their mysteries wrapped in plastic. From the visionary minds of showrunners Mark Frost and David Lynch comes a TV phenomenon 30 years in the making. Welcome to Twin Peaks.

title: Tides of Youth:
subtitle: Two by Shinji Sômai
full_title: Tides of Youth: Two by Shinji Sômai
short_synopsis: Revered by the likes of Ryusuke Hamaguchi and Kiyoshi Kurosawa, Shinji Sômai bottles the rain and the sunshine of youth in his singular coming-of-age films. Naturalistic yet ethereal, these are tender hymns to the magic of summer, a time of introspection and discovery for children and adults alike.
long_synopsis: An influential filmmaker revered by the likes of Ryusuke Hamaguchi and Kiyoshi Kurosawa, the singular Shinji Sômai remains a lesser-known figure internationally compared to his Japanese contemporaries. While his body of work encompasses an eclectic range of styles, it is the coming-of-age genre that makes up the majority of his remarkable output. In these sensitive tales of adolescent conundrums, the rain often descends, not as a drizzle, but in a torrential downpour, cascading into pools of water that reflect the tempestuous emotions of youth. Stormy weather, however, never lasts for long. In the two films in this collection, the sun always returns, as Sômai’s young protagonists take in the changing seasons of life. With her parents getting a divorce, a whirlpool of feelings sends Renko (Tomoko Tabata), the eleven-year-old girl at the heart of Moving (1993), into a tailspin. Unwilling to accept her new reality, she plots to mend the broken marriage through a series of endearingly haywire schemes. In long takes, the camera trails after the hurried footsteps of this mischievous tomboy, now at the threshold of innocence and understanding. In the absence of responsible adults, the rich inner lives of children—filled with unanswered questions and fantastical reveries—comes alive with spellbinding sensitivity. The Friends (1994) is similarly attentive to this invisible life that goes unseen by grownups. Preoccupied with ideas of death, three cheeky boys befriend an elderly recluse in a heartfelt drama that explores aging, regret, and friendship. The film centers on simple acts of care that carry a startlingly transformative effect, highlighting the importance of community as a balm to urban loneliness and isolation. At once naturalistic and ethereal, these are tender hymns to the magic of summer, a time of introspection and discovery for children and adults alike.

title: Queer Histories, Reclaimed:
subtitle: Two by Rob Epstein
full_title: Queer Histories, Reclaimed: Two by Rob Epstein
short_synopsis: Vital archival documents, these two acclaimed documentaries by Rob Epstein bring queer lives, perspectives, and stories away from the edges of the frame and into the light. Seminal works of historical reclamation, they’re alive with the defiant and celebratory energy of Pride Month.
long_synopsis: This Pride Month, we’re looking back to two seminal works of defiant historical reclamation from filmmaker Rob Epstein. Although The Times of Harvey Milk (1984) opens on footage announcing its subject’s 1978 assassination, the Oscar®-winning film vibrates with Milk’s enthusiastic can-do attitude. Charting the extraordinary breadth of the politician’s achievements in a career cut tragically short, Epstein’s moving documentary is also a galvanizing record of community politics in action. The first openly gay elected official in California, Milk encouraged fellow gay and lesbian Americans to come out of the closet, in a crucial first step towards liberation. The same life-affirming movement underpins The Celluloid Closet (1995), codirected by Epstein with collaborator Jeffrey Friedman: taking Hollywood out of the closet, the film points the way toward an openly queer cinema. Based on the groundbreaking book of the same name by activist and film historian Vito Russo, this rich documentary traces an alternative history of Hollywood cinema through its representations of homosexuality. Personal testimony from prominent queer artists and filmmakers combines with thought-provoking film analysis to prove that, despite a frequently hostile culture, gay and lesbian sensibilities have always been part of mainstream American film. Precious archival documents and exhilarating celebrations of unbounded expression, the acclaimed documentaries of this double bill bring queer lives, perspectives, and stories in from the edges of the frame, and proudly into the light.

title: Totally F***ed Up:
subtitle: Two ’90s Cuts by Gregg Araki
full_title: Totally F***ed Up: Two ’90s Cuts by Gregg Araki
short_synopsis: Obsessed with sex yet haunted by mortality, these uninhibited, often outrageous films from Los Angeles icon Gregg Araki spit in the face of boring respectability. Rejecting American conservatism, the teenage dirtbags and anarchic criminals of this double bill are antiheroes of the New Queer Cinema.
long_synopsis: Obsessed with sex yet haunted by mortality, the early films of Los Angeles icon Gregg Araki are among the most daring works of the New Queer Cinema. Like other independent filmmakers of that 1990s movement, Araki messed with the homophobia dominant in America—insulting clichés, persistent taboos—and told uninhibited, often outrageous stories of queer life. Rejecting the policing of queer communities that was rife at the height of the AIDS epidemic, the mischievous, furious films in this double bill give mainstream culture the middle finger. They refuse to be censored or ignored. A rude and raucous cri de coeur against a conservative United States, The Living End (1992) revives early Nouvelle Vague stylings and injects them with horny, gay anarchism. Polar opposites in all but their shared HIV-positive diagnosis, two lovers tear through the roads of the “Land of the Free” on a reckless crime spree. Post-punk, alt-rock, and shoegaze soundtrack their doomed escapade—music of disaffection that also reverberates through Totally F***ed Up (1993). Provocatively described by Araki as “another homo film in fifteen random celluloid fragments,” this splintered portrait of aimless teenage dirtbags harshes the cheap sentiment of other coming-of-age movies. His slackers are the rebels queer American cinema needed in the ’90s, ripping up false optimism with their acid, blasé wit. Both of these cult films have been largely unavailable for decades; now as then, they spit in the face of boring respectability.

title: The Summer Blues
short_synopsis: Sunshine can feel like a balm for the soul. But for the melancholy characters in this collection, the radiance of summer illuminates their blues anew. Basking in fields of ever-changing emotions under beautiful azure skies, these films are as bittersweet—and cathartic—as the best of love songs.
long_synopsis: Sunshine can feel like a balm for the soul. But for the characters in this collection, long summer days mean more time for restless rumination; the radiance of the season illuminates their blues anew. Under the sun, these melancholy few are heavy not only with the sultry air, but also ennui, heartache, and yearning. That combination of heat and longing makes for swooningly atmospheric cinema, in which somber inner landscapes juxtapose with postcard-perfect settings. Basking in fields of ever-changing emotions under beautiful azure skies, the films in this collection are as bittersweet—and cathartic—as the best of love songs.

title: Fake It, Make It:
subtitle: Two by Amalia Ulman
full_title: Fake It, Make It: Two by Amalia Ulman
short_synopsis: With variably resourceful characters faking it until they make it, Amalia Ulman’s cinema is all about the hustle. Immersed in the dog days of late capitalism, her sincerely savvy films give themselves over to the present: scandalous, outrageous absurdities, dizzyingly fun aesthetics, and all.
long_synopsis: Amalia Ulman has an eye for what makes contemporary society so outrageously weird. From ersatz luxury to inspirational “live laugh love” graphics and the internet’s infinite loop of cute cat videos, Ulman is an artist-filmmaker totally switched on to the plasticky fun of the present. Across two idiosyncratic features, she is immersed in the dog days of late capitalism, in which hustlers hold one expedient aphorism close to heart: Faking it is the shortcut to making it in this glittering double bill. Born in late-1980s Argentina, Ulman was raised in Spain, then studied at Central Saint Martins in London, when hipster culture had reached a dizzying height. Fluent in the poses of fellow millennials, she found fame in post-internet art, serially embodying feminine fads on social media and hashtagging them with uncanny sincerity. That heartfelt appreciation for performativity and fickle trend cycles also characterizes her singular work for cinema. Here, variably resourceful characters try to keep up with what’s fashionable and what’s viral—in absurd ways, to hilarious ends. Set among shuttered storefronts of post–financial crash Spain, Ulman’s critically acclaimed debut El Planeta (2021) wears its glamor on a budget. Starring Ulman and her own mama as a daughter and mother with little cash but expensive taste, this thrifty film mixes the postmodern with the classical. Online humor shares screentime with pre-Code Hollywood sensibilities, as unpretentious PowerPoint transitions swipe into the monochrome imagery’s timeless airs. Magic Farm (2024), on the other hand, embraces a Spanish tradition, in Ulman’s own words, of “camp, colorful, queer” cinema. Following an American film crew down to Argentina, this kaleidoscopic film sends up the culture-vulture tendencies of millennial media organizations with fluorescent flair. With a cast that stars “it girl” Chloë Sevigny, this satire of modern scandals—from dangerous pesticides to treacherous sex pests—sparks with Ulman’s distinctive savviness. She is a magpie with a fondness for more than silver: In this economy, you take every shiny item you can get.

title: This Is Not a Coming Out Story
full_title: This Is Not a Coming Out Story
short_synopsis: While coming-out stories are vital to dismantling the celluloid closet, well-worn narratives have also left audiences hungry for more. Liberated from linear thinking around identity, the uncompromising films in this collection are just like their characters: they’re queer, and they’re here to stay.
long_synopsis: The beautiful rainbow of queer experience defiantly resists facile attempts at categorization, and so does queer cinema. With filmmakers breaking free from the celluloid closet, coming-out stories have become undoubtedly vital narratives of film history, yet mainstream cliches surrounding trauma and acceptance have also left audiences hungry for more. In place of all-too-familiar tropes, the bold and audacious films in this collection depict queer lives through a prismatic lens. Turning toward communities and subcultures that exist outside of heteronormative ideals, these complex character portraits do not make queerness their sole focus, but approach it as a facet of vaster emotional terrains and sociopolitical frameworks. From the groundbreaking New Queer Cinema of the 1990s to contemporary arthouse standouts and hybrid experiments, these works are liberated from linear thinking regarding sexuality and identity. Instead of well-trodden cinematic paths, celebrated auteurs make space for surprise and provocation, as tired motifs give way to righteous anger, ecstatic euphoria, and camp chaos. Eclectic in their range of explorations, these uncompromising films are just like their characters: they’re queer, and they’re here to stay.

title: Beyond Binaries:
subtitle: An Orlando Double Bill
full_title: Beyond Binaries: An Orlando Double Bill
short_synopsis: The free spirit of Virginia Woolf’s Orlando thrums through this collection, which offers two singular interpretations of a queer literary classic. Attesting to the enduring legacy of a gender non-conforming icon, these mercurial marvels leap through time and space with dazzling experimentation.
long_synopsis: The liberated spirit of Virginia Woolf’s defiant and playful novel Orlando thrums through this collection, which offers two singular cinematic interpretations of a queer literary classic. Starring Tilda Swinton as the immortal protagonist who miraculously changes their sex, Sally Potter’s eponymous 1992 adaptation ruffles the dusty conventions of the period film. Here, epochs blaze by at dazzling speed. Sumptuous historical costumes by the renowned Sandy Powell are contrasted with experimental and modernist film techniques, such as breaking the fourth wall. Blurring time and space, the juxtaposition of the old and the new erases facile binaries, including those of gender. The contemporary resonance of Woolf’s book is further explored in Paul B. Preciado’s Orlando, My Political Biography (2023). Inviting trans and non-binary people from all walks of life to audition for the titular role, this inventive documentary situates the original text within current sociopolitical issues faced by these communities, such as systematic discrimination and medical injustice. Though different in genre, these mercurial marvels attest to the enduring legacy of a gender non-conforming icon.

title: Let's Eat! Food and Film
short_synopsis: More than mere prop, food is the main event in this collection. We can’t eat the screen, but these films are alive to the cinematic delights to be savored in cooking and eating. From tasty treats to inventive concoctions, this mouth-watering collection offers much to chew on.
long_synopsis: A stew simmers on a hot stove; colorful dishes adorn an overflowing dinner table; faces brighten with delight at the first forkful of a meal: More than mere prop, food is the main event across the films of this collection. We can’t eat the screen, but the directors gathered here are alive to the cinematic pleasures to be savored in cooking and eating. Some luxuriate in the magic of the kitchen. As ingredients transform before our eyes, an appetizing symphony of sizzling, boiling, and chopping quietens the mind and sends the stomach rumbling. Other filmmakers zoom out from the preparation of food to capture atmosphere. Cooking is a meditative, sensual, and intimate experience for some of these protagonists, but a race against time for others. Behind the scenes in busy restaurant kitchens, chefs survive turbulent shifts thanks to the camaraderie bubbling up between cooking stations. Food also brings people together on the other side of swinging kitchen doors. Families and friends in this collection often gather around humble dishes, carefully prepared. Even the simplest meal can be a scrumptious declaration of love. Meanwhile, in more sophisticated circles, extravagant culinary creations attract gourmands who eat to impress; their menus hold glimpses of wealth and ambition. Whether the camera lingers on cornucopian feasts, deconstructed platings, or unassuming treats, these films follow characters for whom food is about more than getting their bellies full. From tasty delicacies to bizarre concoctions, this mouth-watering collection offers much to chew on.

title: Time Out of Mind:
subtitle: Films by Bertrand Bonello
full_title: Time Out of Mind: Films by Bertrand Bonello
short_synopsis: More than modern, the cinema of Bertrand Bonello is visionary. Running through his diverse filmography is an avid curiosity for the ways in which yesterday and tomorrow make themselves felt in the present. Hypnotic and thought-provoking, the films in this collection tune into something in the air.
long_synopsis: Bertrand Bonello’s cinema isn’t of its time—rather, it seems as though it is reaching us from the future. More than simply modern, his films are visionary. Running through his diverse filmography is an avid curiosity for the ways in which yesterday and tomorrow make themselves felt in the present. Always surprising, bold, and atmospheric, his work borrows from a variety of genres and modes, revealing how history unfolds and repeats itself across familiar narratives. Bonello’s films capture reality in limbo. Yet through his playful and attentive camera, this discombobulating state of in-betweenness becomes a site of generative chaos: the twilight of an old world is also the dawn of a new one. The French filmmaker looks on with tenderness and compassion as his characters weather change, adapt to a new normal in constant flux, and imagine better destinies for themselves and others. Hypnotic and thought-provoking, these augural films tune into something in the air.

title: After the End:
subtitle: Postapocalyptic Cinema
short_synopsis: Living among civilization’s ruins, the embattled characters in this collection fight to survive, against the odds and despite the lessons of history. Imagining how survivors of the apocalypse might weather the end times, these films bring us face to face with humanity at its most raw.
long_synopsis: Hope is all that the embattled characters in this collection have left. Adrift among civilization’s ruins, they are surrounded by stark reminders of man’s own vulnerability and fallibility. And yet, against the odds and despite the lessons of history, they still fight tooth and nail to survive. Envisioning how survivors of the apocalypse might weather the end times, the filmmakers gathered here contend with that inexorable drive to go on. Some focus on the often terrifying force of self-preservation instincts—armed with makeshift weapons and on hair-trigger alert, men soon turn into savage beasts. These films make it clear: should a cataclysmic event turn the world upside down, there is no guarantee that things would ever return to normal. Other directors dare to ask: was that dusty old planet even worth saving anyway? In the ashen landscapes of post-apocalyptic fiction, they find the seeds of revolutionary change. Letting their imaginations run wild on the blank canvas left by near-total annihilation, these optimists sketch out surreal alternatives to the world we once knew. Viscerally affecting visions of life on the brink of extinction, these films bring us face to face with humanity at its most raw.

title: Humor and Melancholy:
subtitle: The Cinema of Martín Rejtman
full_title: Humor and Melancholy: The Cinema of Martín Rejtman
short_synopsis: The films of Argentinian auteur Martín Rejtman have the beauty of feeling familiar while at the same time surprising us at every turn. Luminous and delightfully off-kilter, these are comedies of sheer originality—mordant, often tender, and always unpredictable.
long_synopsis: The films of Argentinian auteur and novelist Martín Rejtman have the beauty of feeling familiar while at the same time surprising us at every turn. His feature debut Rapado (1992) is today regarded as the film that launched the New Argentine Cinema, a ’90s wave that revived the national film industry by centering human stories and giving voice to what was previously suppressed. A luminous and delightfully off-kilter body of work, Rejtman’s cinema is imbued with a strong sense of languid melancholy and mordantly deadpan humor. Often compared to the likes of Jim Jarmusch, Aki Kaurismäki and Robert Bresson, Rejtman’s deceptively unadorned yet meticulous style is fuelled by an effortless combination of charm and warmth. These are comedies of sheer originality—mordant, often tender, and always unpredictable.

title: Grand Tour:
subtitle: Cinematic Travelogues
short_synopsis: Away from home, the travelers of this collection reveal more of themselves than usual. Though their journeys vary in mileage, even the shortest is transformative. As Miguel Gomes’s Grand Tour lands on our shores, we’re opening our harbor to other cinematic travelogues from around the world.
long_synopsis: A globe-trotting tale of thwarted love, Miguel Gomes’s  Grand Tour (2024) harks back to a type of cinema as old as the medium itself: the travelogue. As soon as cameras were invented, they were whisked away by pioneers who were eager to fix exotic locales to celluloid. But now as then, directors often capture much more than unfamiliar vistas with their roving machines: the travelers themselves are fascinating, too.
