Whether they take to the road with a specific destination in mind, or simply long to get away from the world they know, the protagonists in this collection all wander far from home. In new surroundings, they tend to reveal more of who they are than they would in their usual haunts. Normally hidden behind everyday habits and routines, telling personal details slip through when those itinerants are on the move, voyaging through postcard-worthy panoramas. Mixing in with the crowds of unknown cities, or cutting a lonely figure against a natural backdrop, some of these characters hope to remain observers. But they rarely return home without carrying a bit of those faraway places within them. Though their journeys vary in mileage, even the shortest is transformative. But you don’t have to go anywhere: the call of the open road reverberates within the well-traveled films of this collection.

title: Animated Desires
short_synopsis: Outrageous, hilarious, NSFW—these irresistible shorts depict desire with titillation and irreverence. Crafting candid visions of sex and lust with hand-painted cels or stop-motion puppets, the animators in this collection are unbounded from realism, and go where few live-action films would dare.
long_synopsis: There is more to sex than the physical act itself. Not for nothing do we tend to use metaphors and similes when describing it, rather than sticking to the facts: sex involves the imagination. Unbound from realism, animation is well placed to visualize the most intimate and wordless sensations in striking, surprising ways. In Desire Pie (1977), Lisa Crafts pictures orgasm as an explosion, followed by a dramatic fade to black. Like most of the films in this collection, this short focuses on women’s experiences of sex, and finds plenty to enjoy besides climax. Here and elsewhere, the successive waves of pleasure preceding la petite mort are represented by psychedelic shapes, sensually swaying across the screen or bursting onto it with excitement. Not that the filmmakers gathered here are always oblique when depicting explicit acts. On the contrary: many of these animators go where few live-action directors would ever dare. Featuring anthropomorphized insect and animal characters, Phoebe Jane Hart’s Bug Diner (2024) crafts an unapologetically NSFW comedy sure to make anyone—vertebrate or otherwise—blush up to their ears or antennas. Repurposing a pornographic scenario, the award-winning film playfully acknowledges that most mysterious element of sensuality: the role of fantasy. Full of creative possibilities, animation can give form to lustful flights of fancy. In Michaela Pavlátová’s Tram (2012), a routine train journey turns into a steamy odyssey for its daydreaming female driver. Suzan Pitt pushes things into more abstract but no less racy territory in Asparagus (1979), a surreal journey into a woman’s erotic visions of the suggestively shaped vegetable. Whether they favor digital techniques or hand-drawn designs, painted cels or stop-motion puppets, the filmmakers in this collection all use animation to circumvent persistent taboos around sex and pleasure. From secret tremors of desire to orgiastic passion, from private self-discovery to arousing connection, these wonderful shorts offer up expansive perspectives on sex, celebrating sensuality as an eye-opening kaleidoscope of experiences and sensations.

title: Our Body, Our Choice
short_synopsis: In the fight for women’s bodily autonomy, cinema has not stayed on the sidelines. Featuring women who unsettle the patriarchy in both public and private spheres, each of the films in this collection is a raised fist, an urgent call for women’s right to control their own bodies.
long_synopsis: In the fight for women’s bodily autonomy, cinema has not stayed on the sidelines. Each of the films in this collection is a raised fist, an urgent call for women’s right to control their own bodies. Going back to the sexual revolutions of the 1960s, feminists have recognised the consciousness-raising power of cinema. From challenging dominant beauty standards and making the case for greater access to healthcare, to questioning harmful power dynamics in the sex industry and agitating for a more fluid understanding of gender, films help to reveal the many overlapping yet distinct arenas where that feminist battle is fought. Focusing on different walks of life, this collection sees women defy societal expectations in various ways. Some of them unsettle the patriarchy through public acts of defiance, as activists marching for equality or workers demanding fair conditions. Others center the female friendships in their lives or explore alternatives to the gender binary. All understand that the personal is political. Together, they envision a world where our bodies are our own.

title: A Piece of the Action
short_synopsis: The action genre has long been perceived as a boys’ club. From the mountains of Corsica to the Moroccan desert, from New York’s Chinatown to the metropolis of Manila, this collection takes a whistle-stop tour around the globe to meet the women of action who are beating the men at their own game.
long_synopsis: The Academy Awards® had been running for more than 80 years before a woman won the prize for Best Director. When Kathryn Bigelow took to the stage in 2010, collecting her Oscar® for The Hurt Locker (2009), she wasn’t just breaking her category’s glass ceiling, she was beating the men—including her fellow nominee, and ex-husband, James Cameron—at their own game. From the pneumatic stars of Hollywood’s 1980s action heyday to the auteur status afforded to such genre mavens as Cameron and George Miller, the action film has long been perceived as a boys’ club. If parity behind the camera for this kinetic brand of cinematic spectacle remains elusive for now, that doesn’t mean there aren’t some women filmmakers steadily perfecting the art of the big-screen knuckle sandwich. From the mountains of Corsica to the Moroccan desert, from New York’s Chinatown to the metropolis of Manila, this collection takes a whistle-stop tour around the globe to meet some of the women who are fighting for their own piece of the action.

title: Will They?
subtitle: Won't They?
short_synopsis: For the wandering hearts in this collection, romance can blossom at first sight, or it can take its sweet time to grow. Led astray by destiny, mixed signals, and emotional indecision, these lovers in limbo must find their own ways into each other’s arms. Will they, or won’t they?
long_synopsis: Love is as unpredictable as the weather: it can blossom from the first meeting of the eyes, or it can take months, weeks, if not years to grow. In cinema, the pleasure of watching a romance so often hinges on the suspense of attraction and the thrill of the chase. The paramours in this collection are led astray by mixed signals, emotional indecision, and tricky circumstances. Will they find their own way into each other’s arms, or won’t they? Tempering the intensity of desire with sweet pangs of doubt, these love stories in limbo are worth rooting for.

title: House of NWR:
subtitle: Two Ads by Nicolas Winding Refn
full_title: House of NWR: Two Ads by Nicolas Winding Refn
short_synopsis: Much like the luxury brands that commissioned the films in this collection, Nicolas Winding Refn lets his style do the talking. Including the first commercial to ever play in the Venice Film Festival’s official program, our double bill pairs two eye-popping ads from the Danish provocateur.
long_synopsis: “Style is a way to say who you are without having to speak,” Miuccia Prada once said. The grande dame of fashion could have been talking about the films of Nicolas Winding Refn, director of Drive (2011) and Only God Forgives (2013), whose protagonists have a reticent way with words. For NWR and his laconic antiheroes, style does the talking. Over the past three decades, the Danish provocateur has cultivated a distinct visual language; it’s little wonder that Prada came calling. Tasked with summoning a unique visual identity for the fashion house’s spring/summer 2023 womenswear show, Refn turned in an eye-popping short film that shattered the boundaries between cinema and advertising. Pulsing with NWR’s signature shades of neon, and backed by a throbbing electronic score by frequent collaborator Cliff Martinez, Touch of Crude sees its Prada-clad protagonists unlock a multiverse of menacing, ambient spaces. Refn took the pursuit of pleasure as the driving theme of his 2024 advert for Italian motorcycle brand MV Agusta. Exploring the line between sin and salvation, and inspired by the fetishistic work of Kenneth Anger, Beauty Is Not a Sin finds aesthetic sublimity in its object of desire. “It’s an extension of where all my work is moving towards,” said Refn of the commercial, the first to ever play in the Venice Film Festival’s official program. “Whoever said a movie can’t be seven minutes long?”

title: Hand-picked by Mati Diop
short_synopsis: Winner of the Golden Bear at the 2024 Berlinale, Mati Diop’s Dahomey  is a vital work of cinematic imagination. To celebrate the arrival of this prismatic hybrid documentary, the French filmmaker has curated a trio of dazzling shorts that spark a conversation with her spellbinding feature.
long_synopsis: Winner of the Golden Bear at the 2024 Berlinale, Mati Diop’s Dahomey  is a vital work of cinematic imagination. To celebrate the arrival of this prismatic hybrid documentary, the French filmmaker has curated a trio of dazzling shorts that spark a conversation with her spellbinding feature. You Hide Me (Nii Kwate Owoo, 1970) “Shot at the British Museum in 1970, You Hide Me is the graduation film of a 26-year-old Ghanaian student. Watching this cinematic manifesto for the first time, I kept wondering how director Nii Kwate Owoo had managed to access the underground reserves of the museum––home to 70,000 African artefacts––knowing how strictly inaccessible they were. I myself would be refused access to those of the Quai Branly for the shooting of Dahomey, fifty years later. In fact, this is the film’s real tour de force: by keeping any negotiation off-screen, the filmmaker offers the spectator a space to speculate on the presence of these two Black bodies, both as real as a mirage. In the end, this cathartic performance acts on our unconscious, inviting us to transgress through the limited yet infinite channels of imagination.” L’avance (Djiby Kebe, 2024) “In the history of cinema, some films represent a turning point. For me, L’avance isn’t just a debut short film, but also ground zero for a new generation of Afro-descendants who are only now getting access to fields previously reserved for white people. It was in Paris, where he was born, that Djiby Kebe sketched this twenty-minute, hyper-lucid portrait of a young artist (modelled after himself) in an art world thirsty for young Black blood. A scene that particularly touched me is one in which the artist, Aliou, meets a neighborhood friend shooting a TikTok video, who asks him who he truly is. Aliou says he doesn’t know and that he will be searching for the answer for the rest of his life. L’avance marks the entry of the “Glissantien” anti-hero––named after the Martinican poet and philosopher Édouard Glissant––into contemporary French cinema." Atlantiques (Mati Diop, 2009) “Atlantiques is the first short film that I shot in Dakar. I filmed Serigne, a young man who has just been repatriated from Spain having braved the crossing from the Senegalese coast by boat. For the first time, he shared his epic journey with his two friends (Alpha and Cheikh, my cousin), who were trying hard to convince him to stay. But Serigne was already gone. Whatever the cost, he was determined to jump on a boat again. By the time I had chosen to displace my cinema from Paris to Dakar, an entire generation was escaping the country in the opposite direction (for Europe) by sea, where many of them perished. I understood much later that we were all under a spell that would not say its name.”

title: Try the Substance
short_synopsis: If you are enjoying The Substance, then you could be a candidate for an increased intake. Good news! The next kit has been delivered: an extra set of switches, stabilizers, and activators, designed to enhance cinephile credentials. Click through for access. A better self is just moments away.
long_synopsis: Are you dreaming of a better self? If you are enjoying The Substance, then you could be a good candidate for an increased intake: an exclusive set of switches, stabilizers, and activators to enhance your cinephile credentials. From cult classics to midnight movies, these films dare to venture into the twisted depths of our psyches, remaking mind and flesh. You won’t be the same after watching these films. Click through. Your next hit is just moments away.

title: You Up?:
subtitle: Midnight Movies
short_synopsis: Prepare to enter a world of vice and excess with this erotically charged collection of midnight movies. From steamy examinations of amour fou to fevered tales of transgression from some of cinema’s great iconoclasts, these sexually explicit films tap into the dark side of desire.
long_synopsis: Prepare to enter a world of vice and excess with this erotically charged collection of midnight movies. Capturing the spirit of purple pictures that play on television in the small hours of the night, these licentious modern greats inhabit a nocturnal realm that extends far beyond the watershed. From steamy examinations of amour fou to fevered tales of transgression from some of cinema’s great iconoclasts, these sexually explicit films tap into the dark side of desire. Don’t forget to draw the curtains—there are no boundaries that the films in this collection are afraid to cross.

title: Diamonds in the Rough:
subtitle: Two by Sean Baker
full_title: Diamonds in the Rough: Two by Sean Baker
short_synopsis: Sean Baker’s American misfits lead risky, adventurous lives—and his spirited films take us along for the ride. Embracing the good times that break raucously through convention and hardship, this double bill finds fun in freedom, and plays like a breath of fresh air.
long_synopsis: Characters in Sean Baker’s films sometimes speak before they think. In his explosive, iPhone-shot sensation Tangerine (2015), the rip-roaring action is set off by one woman unwisely telling her friend that her lover is a cheat. Kids in the candy-colored The Florida Project (2017), meanwhile, do not always know how offensive their expletives are. Yet for all their rudeness and blundering, we’d happily follow Baker’s protagonists wherever their adventures might lead. Brave and determined, these raucous individuals do things their own way, never wasting time trying to be anybody but themselves. Living on the margins of society, Baker’s characters may not conform to social rules, but they grapple with the same desire for happiness as anyone else. Transgender sex workers in Tangerine move through a world of social rejects who are, in many ways, more virtuous than apparently upstanding citizens; these outcasts find moments of joy in friendship and solidarity. As for the rambunctious Moonee in The Florida Project: her childhood in a highway motel isn’t typical, but it’s happy all the same. Baker’s compassionate cinema emphasizes the good times that his hilarious and eminently likable misfits uncover outside the bounds of convention. While astute on the injustices that surround sex work, his films never reduce them to statistics, embracing life’s vagaries instead. Finding fun in freedom, this spirited double bill plays like a breath of fresh air.

title: Playing with Time:
subtitle: Films by Raúl Ruiz and Valeria Sarmiento
full_title: Playing with Time: Films by Raúl Ruiz and Valeria Sarmiento
short_synopsis: Once wandering in a state of exile, the unfinished projects left behind by Chilean maestro Raúl Ruiz have gained a poignant afterlife, thanks to his widow and collaborator Valeria Sarmiento. Dreamily surreal and deeply political, his singular vision lives on in these posthumously finished works.
long_synopsis: Having made more than 100 films during his lifetime, Chilean maestro Raúl Ruiz left behind a treasure trove of unfinished works after his death in 2011. Once wandering in a state of exile, some of these projects have now been rescued and revitalized, thanks to the tireless preservation efforts of Ruiz’s widow and creative partner Valeria Sarmiento. Supported by the Chilean production company Poetastros, her continuing collaboration with Ruiz’s oeuvre marks a poignant extension of the filmmaker’s legacy. Across these posthumously released titles, Ruiz’s dreamily uncanny and deeply political vision lives on. Released six years after his death, The Wandering Soap Opera (2017) was shot by Ruiz in 1990 and later edited and completed by Sarmiento. Marking Ruiz’s return to Chile after the fall of Pinochet, the film playfully reconfigures the visual language of soap operas. It spins a web of surreal vignettes, each one full of droll archetypes. If The Wandering Soap Opera carries the wisdom of an older artist, The Tango of the Widower and Its Distorting Mirror (2020) takes us back to the very beginning of Ruiz’s career for what would have been his feature debut. Shot in black and white, the original 1967 material is resurrected under Sarmiento’s supervision through editing and dialogue reconstruction. A sense of the spectral lingers over this story, which revolves around a professor tormented by his wife’s death. Oscillating between the grotesque and the melancholic, his descent into madness erupts into visions of crawling wigs and gibberish chants, hallucinatory images steeped in Dadaist logic. This atmosphere of disorder continues in Socialist Realism (2023), a docufiction shot and shelved by Ruiz following the 1973 Chilean coup. Reconstructed by Sarmiento, the final film provides an acerbic look at Salvador Allende’s Popular Unity government, and the distance between its left-wing idealism and the plight of the working class. A testament to the powerful artistic bond between Ruiz and Sarmiento, the existence of these three films is nothing short of a cinematic miracle.

title: A New You:
subtitle: Three by Coralie Fargeat
full_title: A New You:  Three by Coralie Fargeat
short_synopsis: Seduced by the thought of a new you? Change is a double-edged sword in The Substance and two other films by Coralie Fargeat. Sparking debate with trenchant social critique and body horror innovations, the French director gets under the skin of contemporary culture with extreme daring and insight.
long_synopsis: Seduced by the thought of a new you? Just know that change can be a double-edged sword. While some characters are empowered in Coralie Fargeat’s daring cinema of modern metamorphoses, others descend into nightmare. What starts as a dream in The Substance (2024) soon descends into delirium, as the French filmmaker injects Hollywood with a hyper-reality check. Starring the iconic Demi Moore in a bold, career-high performance, this exhilarating film shines a lurid spotlight on misogynist discourse around aging and beauty. It’s not only #MeToo rage channeled into ultra-contemporary cinema—it’s an anarchic midnight movie for the 21st century. Before The Substance, Fargeat hardwired her body horror innovations into a Cinderella short story and a delirious revenge shocker. Similarly tapping into a contemporary desire for self-optimization, these two films stun with impressive alter egos. Suddenly serious contenders on the brutal battlegrounds of sex and dating, these upgraded characters are beautiful, dangerous machines. Catalyzed by a speculative technology that overhauls the user’s ordinary appearance, Reality+ (2014) reveals the superficial impulses of our image-obsessed society. Switching pumpkin carriages and glass slippers for a brain chip with finite charge, this futuristic fairy tale is The Substance in miniature, rupturing romantic ideals and sci-fi fantasies in less than 30 minutes. Things get bloodier, dirtier, and infinitely more fiery in the harsh desert landscape of Revenge (2017). Critically acclaimed for tricking out exploitation tropes to neo-feminist ends, Fargeat’s feature debut is another enthralling tale of radical change. Going from Barbie to badass in an excruciating rebirth—but holding onto an iconic pink-star earring all the while—the killer heroine of this film fires on all cylinders. Whether for better or worse, the three films in this collection activate extreme transformations to electrifying cinematic effect.

title: Not Just a Girl
short_synopsis: Read as “difficult” in the eyes of the patriarchy, the young women in our collection carve out their own paths against a myriad of sexist hurdles. Fueled by fierce determination, these plucky heroines form a radical vision of womanhood, one that refuses to bend to the controlling gaze of others.
long_synopsis: In a world where certain expectations of femininity are still thrust upon women, the possibility of liberation from societal norms is a tricky one. Instead of being molded into ladylike ideals, the plucky heroines in our collection dare to carve out their own paths. Their battles in these decades-spanning films are stunningly varied. From the hurdles of workplace discrimination to economic hardships and racial prejudices, these powerful characters face the obstacles of the day with inspiring determination. Read as “difficult” in the eyes of the patriarchy, their actions here instead signal a rousing desire to break free from sexist constraints. Emphasizing the perspectives of young women, these formidable performances form a radical vision of womanhood, one that refuses to bend the controlling gaze of others.

title: Modern Lovers
short_synopsis: Does the guy get the girl? Do they live happily ever after? The films in this collection have no time for such clichés. Getting into the complexities of real life, they expand the cinematic ABCs of love and boldly shatter convention, revealing the many different forms that modern romance can take.
long_synopsis: Does the guy get the girl? Do they kiss at the end and live happily ever after? The films in this collection have no time for such clichés. Getting into the complexities of real life, they expand the cinematic ABCs of love and audaciously shatter convention, revealing the many different forms that modern romance can take. While other filmmakers pan timidly away from the action during sex scenes, Gaspar Noé makes it the heart of Love (2015), a provocatively titled chronicle of a profoundly carnal liaison. Marija Kavtaradzė’s Slow (2023), meanwhile, offers an altogether different picture of intimacy. Through its sensitive depiction of asexual romance, it calmly and confidently untangles companionship from sensual connection. Physical or otherwise, affection is a universal need. Seemingly destined for a life of loneliness, the titular divorcée in Sebastián Lelio’s exuberant character study Gloria (2013) is determined to find erotic and emotional satisfaction. She faces gendered expectations that echo those of the adulterous heroine in Monia Chokri’s new-age The Nature of Love (2023). Playing with rom-com tropes, this offbeat film ponders the mysteries of attraction, partnership, and passion. Redefining romance for the 21st century, each of the films in this collection bravely explores the enigmas of that crazy little thing called love.

title: Chasing Rainbows:
subtitle: A Ross Brothers Double Bill
full_title: Chasing Rainbows: A Ross Brothers Double Bill
short_synopsis: Abrading the line between fiction and nonfiction filmmaking, this collection takes to the road with the Ross Brothers for a journey into the heart of the American experience. Insightful, empathetic, and searingly authentic, these enchanting films are all-embracing odes to communities lost and found.
long_synopsis: A van carrying five teenagers hurtles toward a distant horizon, chasing the promise of America. Here, in Bill Ross IV and Turner Ross’ Gasoline Rainbow (2023), a community is forged from a joint passed, a shoulder leaned on, and a concord of voices raised in song. It’s an odyssey of discovery for cast, filmmakers, and viewers alike; the latest in a cinematic project that has continually abraded the line between fiction and nonfiction filmmaking. An all-embracing compendium of their hometown of Sidney, Ohio, the Ross Brothers’ first feature 45365 (2009) is the epicenter of an oeuvre that interrogates the American experience through the microcosm of community. But Sidney couldn’t contain the duo’s peregrinating curiosity, and neither could the traditional parameters of documentary. With Gasoline Rainbow, the siblings combined a predetermined structure with the spontaneity of improvisation. If 45365 marked the starting point of the brothers’ formal exploration, the later film could hardly be called a destination. Instead, it’s a stopover on the road less traveled. Charged with a state of flux, the films in this double bill don’t deal in closure. For the Ross Brothers, much like the teens in Gasoline Rainbow, every ending is a new beginning, with unmapped frontiers on the other side of that distant horizon.

title: Besties Only
short_synopsis: Friendship is central to the films in this collection, which assembles various cinematic examples of that most powerful bond. Jointly facing whatever life throws at them, these onscreen friends draw strength from one another, finding refuge in the private world they have lovingly created.
long_synopsis: Friendship is at the heart of the films in this collection, which gathers together various cinematic examples of that most powerful bond. In many comedies and dramas, friends are a welcome shoulder to cry on, offering support through tough times and making the good ones even better. But a friend can be a bad influence too. Drawing on that dangerous dynamic, some filmmakers follow the more sordid impulses behind platonic entanglements and take us down darker paths. For those films that are not cautionary tales, friendship is celebrated as a complicated dance of autonomy and dependency. Its ups and downs — from joyful adventures to teary arguments —also help many of the buddies in the films assembled here to grow up. Through the love of those who know them best, they come ever closer to being the person they were meant to become. These onscreen friends find strength and courage in their close connections, jointly facing whatever life throws at them and finding refuge in the private world they have created together.

title: Ira Sachs:
subtitle: The Early Years
short_synopsis: Ira Sachs is one of our favorite filmmakers, and has an astounding back catalog to confirm it. Shimmering performances, fragile erotics, and candid encounters in moving shades of blue dazzle and wound across these three films, glistening with an intimate understanding of human desire.
long_synopsis: In 1990s New York, a restless subject drifts about an apartment, one corner to another. She lies down, glamor on pause, and asks Ira Sachs what he is looking for, filming her with that camera. “Just yourself,” the novice director answers. She shows him herself—multiple selves that clash as much as constellate, obscure as much as disclose—and sets the tone for a filmography in which identity is candid, mutable, wayward. That Sachs had an affinity with the messy humanists and combustible melodramas of European cinema—Jean Eustache, Rainer Werner Fassbinder, Maurice Pialat—was evident right from the start. After a capricious character study in that short film, Lady (1993), Sachs’s first feature rippled with similar fluidity and ambiguity. Back in his hometown of Memphis, he made a groundbreaking intervention into New Queer Cinema with The Delta (1996). Casting issues of race and class into the sticky morass of sexuality, this singular coming-of-age film immerses itself in cruising’s fickle waters, where the narrative focus becomes gradually unmoored. Delicately filmed on 16mm stock, the grain of the image is bathed in evocative blues: the electric cobalt of police sirens and the soft periwinkle of boxer shorts combine in a promiscuous palette, expressive of both danger and tender touch. That color becomes exquisite melancholy in Forty Shades of Blue (2006). Winner of the Grand Jury Prize at Sundance, this shattering romance is, like Lady and The Delta, about an interloper in white middle-class America and shimmers with an astonishing lead performance. Whether an isolated woman of ethereal detachment or a charismatic lover with mercurial desires, these characters open up under Sachs’s direction. What’s the secret? He asks everything and nothing of them. He asks only that they be themselves.

title: Must We Mean What We Say?:
subtitle: Two by Kit Zauhar
full_title: Must We Mean What We Say?:  Two by Kit Zauhar
short_synopsis: Writer, director, and actor Kit Zauhar knows that actual people often speak at cross purposes; her excruciating, but engrossing dialogue gets to the mucked-up heart of modern relationships. She’s the talk of the town for good reason, spiking her first films with piercing insight and devastating wit.
long_synopsis: Surrounded by film students who were overinflated with pretension and self-seriousness, Kit Zauhar soon decided she would be a different kind of director. “I do not have an MFA or a favorite scene from The Godfather because I’ve never watched it and I probably never will,” reads one offhand confession on her personal website. Almost an artist’s statement, it’s spiked with her irreverence toward old canons and other ostensible authorities. Zauhar idolized few, and learned instead from scrappier independent filmmakers: the first trick was to make use of both her body and her voice as resources, to put herself in the frame. Writing, directing, and acting in Actual People (2021) and This Closeness (2023), Zauhar has become the talk of the town for her perception and quick wit—not to mention what she can achieve with a wisp of a budget. Wavering between college and life, between hometown Philadelphia and impressive New York, characters in these films are recognisable, if not relatable in any cozy kind of way. Nice people, Zauhar has commented in interviews, are just not that interesting. Admit it, these films nudge: the flawed and the failing among us make for more engrossing dramas, and some excruciating laughs. Drawn to how language can be a blunt, inelegant tool in one moment and a lacerating weapon in the next, Zauhar manipulates mutual understanding into a knotted process of mucking up and making up. She knows that real people speak out of spite, at cross purposes, and out of their asses—and that rare insight makes for riveting, poignant, often hilarious cinema.

title: Apocalypse Now:
subtitle: Four Sci-Fi Parables by Piotr Szulkin
full_title: Apocalypse Now: Four Sci-Fi Parables by Piotr Szulkin
short_synopsis: One of the great visionaries of European cinema, Piotr Szulkin’s films are provocative allegories one and all. Made while Poland was under the thumb of Soviet totalitarianism, these potent works of science fiction combine righteous sociopolitical fury with the ample pleasures of genre.
long_synopsis: One of the great visionaries of European cinema, Piotr Szulkin was a useful thorn in the side of the Polish authorities. His 1981 masterpiece War of the Worlds: Next Century was, like all of his major films, ostensibly a work of science fiction. Deemed a provocative attack on the authoritarian regime in the months leading up to the declaration of martial law, the film was pulled from its competition slot at the Cannes Film Festival. A satire of media manipulation set in the days following a martian invasion, it would later be revealed that the very same authorities who had denied its release had been studying the film as an instructional tool for social control. Faced with such ironies, it’s little wonder that Szulkin rebuffed straightforward categorisation of his work. “I do not make sci-fi,” he said, “but rather borrow from its aesthetic… Film is a scream… my scream, and an expression of my need to confront the situation we had found ourselves in.” The situation in question was Communist rule, and all four of the films in this collection—informally known as his “Apocalyptic Tetralogy” or “Tetralogy of Doom”—were made while Poland was under the thumb of Soviet totalitarianism. Inspired by the likes of Franz Kafka and George Orwell, Pier Paolo Pasolini and H.G. Wells, Szulkin’s works of future-noir are potent allegories one and all—not just for contemporary Polish society, but for a global age marked by atomic anxiety and widespread distrust in monolithic power structures. Yet for all the sociopolitical subtext of his cinema, Szulkin retains the ample pleasures of genre. These are films populated with smoky bars and femme fatales, shadow-dwelling villains and, in Golem (1980), lab-grown doppelgängers. They’re also marvels of utilitarian production design, threaded with dark veins of mordant humor—not for nothing has Ga-Ga: Glory to the Heroes (1985) been compared to Terry Gilliam’s Brazil (1985). In the apocalypse according to Szulkin, the world ends with neither a bang nor a whimper, but with an insistence—as the world burns—that everything is fine.

title: The Next Generation:
subtitle: British Debuts
short_synopsis: Like a breath of fresh air, a new generation of directors are heralding an exciting era of British filmmaking with their audacious works. Highlighting some of the best debuts in recent years, our collection champions the emerging talents who are changing the world of cinema, one film at a time.
long_synopsis: Like a breath of fresh air, a new generation of directors are heralding an exciting era of British filmmaking with their bold and daring works. Full of surprises, this wave of up-and-comers are reshaping not only stylistic conventions, but also narrative devices. From the challenges faced by young people to timeless questions of identity, these compelling stories come from all walks of life, encompassing a diverse range of genres. Highlighting some of the most exciting British debuts in recent years, our collection champions the emerging talents who are changing the world of cinema, one film at a time.

title: Films for Gross Girls:
subtitle: Three by Luna Carmoon
full_title: Films for Gross Girls: Three by Luna Carmoon
short_synopsis: Young English talent Luna Carmoon isn’t one to gloss over the icky growing pains of girlhood—in fact, she likes to make them even more gross. Across the zesty and bittersweet films in this collection, the bold director vividly captures the textures and convulsions of adolescence, zits and all.
long_synopsis: Luna Carmoon isn’t one to gloss over the icky growing pains of girlhood—in fact, the South Londoner likes to make them even more gross. Though girlish bows and pink frills are present in the director’s films, so are messy rooms, blemished skin, smudged makeup, and other sticky imperfections of adolescence. Her rebellious characters resist the rules of propriety, embracing instead all that is physical, profane, and disgusting about being a girl. In an interview with Sight and Sound, Carmoon said it was always the case that as soon as she got behind a camera, she was “going to make films for gross girls.” Embroiled in a toxic relationship, the two friends in Nosebleed (2018) experiment with the limits and tensions of female friendship. Unconditional love turns into cruelty, affection into aggression, a gift into poison. It’s a surreal, thrilling gut punch of a film that announced Carmoon as a singular talent to watch. Turning her unflinching gaze to the charged years of sexual awakenings, Shagbands (2020) highlights the manifold threats that stalk girls as they come of age. But the short also points to the unadulterated freedom and unyielding support they find in one another, away from the judgment of others. In Hoard (2023), Carmoon’s debut feature and her most uninhibited work yet, the boldness of a gross girl inspires ire but also jealousy in those around her. A vivid evocation of the obsessive and compulsive temperaments of young girls everywhere, the film zooms in on the sensual hunger integral to their age: it’s a film you can almost smell and touch. Across these three zesty and bittersweet works, Luna Carmoon captures the textures and convulsions of girlhood, zits and all.

title: A Place of Our Own:
subtitle: Queer Spaces on Film
full_title: A Place of Our Own: Queer Spaces on Film
short_synopsis: Comprising timeless classics and lesser-known gems, the eclectic collection offers a window onto a constellation of queer spaces. Mapping out an atlas of desire from bars, nightclubs, cruising spots, and more, here are sites of joy, pleasure, and resistance. Come on in, and stay a while.
long_synopsis: Famously used by LGBTQ+ activists in the 1970s, the defiant call of solidarity "out of the closets and into the streets’’ powerfully evokes how the queer experience is also a spatial one. Comprising timeless classics and lesser-known gems, this eclectic collection offers a window onto a constellation of queer spaces. Mapping out an atlas of desire from bars, nightclubs, cruising spots, and more, here are sites of joy, pleasure, and resistance. Reclaiming a piece of forgotten queer heritage, Tzor Edery and Tom Prezman’s animated documentary short Maurice’s Bar (2023) movingly resurrects Paris’s second-ever gay bar from its tragic fate under the Nazi occupation of France. As seen with the transgender haven at the center of Camila José Donoso’s Casa Roshell (2017), bars are a place of sanctuary, revolution, and fantasy. Nightclubs, on the other hand, shake with a different energy. A touchstone of queer cinema, Jennie Livingston’s vibrant documentary Paris Is Burning (1990) takes in the glamor and reveals the rules, traditions, and struggles of the ballroom scene in 1980s New York. Leilah Weinraub’s Shakedown (2018) is just as dynamic, chronicling a series of lesbian strip-nights in 2000s Los Angeles with unvarnished authenticity. When taking time off from the dancefloor, both films observe the intimate, familial bonds that blossom within the Black queer community, whose experiences are still sorely underrepresented onscreen. Injecting a dose of private desire into public space, the act of cruising is simultaneously fraught with the promise of pleasure and the risk of discovery. Here body language takes over, and a simple exchange of gazes can speak louder than words. Alongside outdoor places such as beaches or parks, the public washroom is a significant cruising hotspot, appearing prominently in films such as Zhang Yuan’s landmark East Palace, West Palace (1996) and Nans Laborde-Jourdàa’s surreal Boléro (2023). Evading surveillance and punishment is thoughtfully explored in Onyeka Igwe and Huw Lemmey’s Ungentle (2022), which traces an unexpected connection between cruising and espionage. Even restrictive places such as prisons or boarding schools are transformed in queer cinema, as homoerotic passions rebel against systematic dogmas. Looking beyond the filmic frame, these treasures are a vital reminder of the importance of queer spaces. Come on in, and stay a while.

title: Just for One Day
short_synopsis: David Bowie was on to something when he sang we can be heroes / just for one day. Immersed in the lives of their characters for 24 hours or less, these momentous films throw the ordinary and the mundane to the wind. Change can strike like lightning. Here is a cinema electrified by that possibility.
long_synopsis: Was David Bowie thinking of the ghoul-fighting characters in Night of the Living Dead (1968) when he sang we can be heroes / just for one day in 1977? Not quite!—but that sentiment courses throughout George A. Romero’s tremendous horror classic and the one-day wonders of this collection. Immersed in the lives of workers, superstars, and outcasts for 24 hours or less, these films throw the ordinary and the mundane to the wind, in the knowledge that change can strike like lightning. Bold comedies such as Emma Seligman’s Shiva Baby (2020) and Quentin Dupieux’s Yannick (2023) need no more than a handful of hours and a gathering of people for heart-stopping shock and hilarious surprise. Agnès Varda’s Cléo from 5 to 7 (1962), Joachim Trier’s Oslo, August 31 (2011), and Sean Baker’s Tangerine (2015), meanwhile, open up the quotidian to profound encounter by freely wandering the neighborhoods of Paris, Oslo, and Los Angeles. While the protagonists of Aneil Karia’s Surge (2020) and Tom Ford’s A Single Man (2009) are worlds apart, both are seized from the crushed brink of disillusionment to turn life around in a circadian cycle. Rome wasn’t built in a day, they say—narrow-minded defeatists, the lot of them! Not a second is wasted in these momentous films. Prepare to come out the other side transformed and inspired.

title: Priscilla:
subtitle: Beyond the Frame
short_synopsis: Who better to document the making of a film about a teenager than a teenager herself? Coupled with Sofia Coppola’s Priscilla, Liv McNeil’s making-of documentary paints an inspiring picture of visual artistry and creative collaboration, from one generation of women filmmakers to another.
long_synopsis: With her latest feature, the stylish and heartbreaking Priscilla, Sofia Coppola brings to screen the untold stories of an icon while also returning to a subject central to her acclaimed filmography: the inner lives of young girls. And who better to document the making of that film about a teenager than a teenager herself? Shot when she was only seventeen, Liv McNeil’s engrossing documentary sparkles with an infectious enthusiasm for the magic of cinema. Through her keen gaze, we observe the marvelous ways in which 1950s Germany and 1960s Memphis are meticulously erected on a soundstage. In addition to interviews with Jacob Elordi, Cailee Spaeny, and Priscilla Presley herself, the film also grants a window into Coppola’s directorial process, which is rigorous and playful all at once. Seen together, Coppola’s Priscilla and McNeil’s behind-the-scenes short paint an inspiring picture of visual artistry and creative collaboration, from one generation of women filmmakers to another.

title: Between the Lines:
subtitle: Writers on Screen
full_title: Between the Lines: Writers on Screen
short_synopsis: What goes on between bookish lines? Are blank pages so terrifying? How do darlings get killed? Aspiring novelists revel in lively literary circles and soulful poets devote themselves to the wonders of the everyday in this collection, which casts light on the ambitious, imaginative lives of writers.
long_synopsis: Strange creatures, writers—and no two are quite the same. Scribblers of all kinds are at work across this collection, in which writing proves to be, for the reclusive perfectionist as much as the hedonistic socialite, so much more than words. Inked not on paper but on skin, the unorthodox writing of Peter Greenaway’s dynamically erotic The Pillow Book (1995) bypasses the common fear of the blank page. Charlie Kaufman’s anxious avatar in Adaptation. (2002) has no such luck. Wrangling words onto new documents remains an obstinate problem for the fretful protagonist in this meta-film, which wallows hilariously in the screenwriter’s jealousy and insecurity. Aspiring novelists shine in Stephen Fry’s Bright Young Things (2003) and Joachim Trier’s Reprise (2006). Seas and decades apart, the literary circles of 1930s London and contemporary Oslo nonetheless resemble one another in these Künstlerroman tales, as ambition frolics with revelry. In two films by American indie icons, the soulfulness of ramshackle poets comes instead to the fore. Different in many ways, the New Jersey bus driver in Jim Jarmusch’s Paterson (2016) and the Florida Keys drop out in Harmony Korine’s The Beach Bum (2019) both take solace, even so, in meter and verse. Women also pick up authorial pens in this tome of a collection. Josephine Decker’s off-kilter biopic Shirley (2020) immerses itself in the shadowy mind of horror writer Shirley Jackson, while the pensive filmmaker in Mia Hansen-Løve’s Bergman Island (2021) reflects on what it is to be among directorial giants and still remain an artist in one’s own right. Between the lines of books, poems, and scripts, the manifold lives of writers are so many volumes of thought and emotion, just waiting to be put into words.

title: David Lynch:
subtitle: Delusions and dreams
short_synopsis: David Lynch’s art manifests itself as an eruption of his subconscious. Fascinated by the darkness that lurks beyond the diaphanous facades of the American psyche, his alluring nightmares oscillate between beauty and chaos, collectively forming an ecstatic body of work unlike anything else in cinema.
long_synopsis: It’s hard to think of another filmmaker as attuned to the avant-garde who has made such a substantial mainstream impact as David Lynch. So recognizably singular, yet defiant of categorization is his cinema that “Lynchian” has become a ubiquitous shorthand for anything that straddles the uncanny perimeters of dream and reality, resisting the marked boundaries of definition. A multidisciplinary artist whose work encompasses everything from painting and sculpture to video installations and film, Lynch is a rare example of a filmmaker who has been exhibited in galleries, cinemas, online and on television, without compromising the integrity of his sui generis vision. Lynch’s art, across multiple media, appears to manifest itself as an eruption of his subconscious. His feature films may flirt with traditional storytelling methods, such as plot and character development, but they’re invariably untethered, adrift in a slipstream of dream logic and psychological quicksand. Fascinated by the darkness that lurks beyond the diaphanous, apple-pie facades of the American psyche, the alluring nightmares of David Lynch oscillate between beauty and chaos, collectively forming an ecstatic body of work unlike anything else in modern cinema.

title: Calling the Shots:
subtitle: Women Cinematographers
short_synopsis: Many of the most unforgettable images in film history have been shot by women cinematographers who, against all odds, have left a lasting mark on the big screen. Celebrating their contribution to cinema, this collection looks beyond the beautiful frame to honor the unsung figures behind the lens.
long_synopsis: From the early days of cinema, female cinematographers have played a pivotal role in shaping the visual language of the medium. From sweeping epics to intimate dramas, studio projects to avant-garde experiments, their sizable impact on the seventh art extends across different countries and eras. And yet their tremendous contributions are still sorely overlooked compared to those of their male counterparts. Although women have gained more visibility within the industry in recent years, cinematography remains at the bottom of the list when it comes to female representation in film. In their study of the 250 films that topped the US box office in 2022, the Celluloid Ceiling (which tracks employment statistics for women in Hollywood) reported that only 7% of those features had female cinematographers—a mere 3% increase from 1998. As for the Academy Awards®, only two women have been nominated for Best Cinematography in the 96 years since its conception. From sexist stereotypes to limited resources, the challenges faced by camerawomen are numerous and systematic. At the same time, some of the most breathtaking and unforgettable images captured on film have been shot by women who, against all odds, have left a lasting mark on the big screen. As joint forces, female cinematographers and directors have ushered in contemporary classics, as seen in the fruitful partnerships between Hélène Louvart and Alice Rohrwacher, Claire Mathon and Céline Sciamma. Following in their creative footsteps are the likes of Ashley Connor (Madeline’s Madeline): rising stars whose accomplishments signal the promising ascent of the next generation of camerawomen. This collection celebrates the unsung female cinematographers who lent their powerful gaze to some of the most loved and acclaimed works in movie history. Eclectic in their span and subjects, these films beckon us not only to behold the beautiful frame, but to also honor the women behind the lens.

title: Around the World:
subtitle: The International Oscar
full_title: Around the World: The International Oscar
short_synopsis: Dialogue may be spoken in myriad tongues, but the language of cinema is universal. This collection brings together some of the greatest films to have either won or been nominated for Best International Feature Film at the Academy Awards®.
long_synopsis: “Once you overcome the one-inch tall barrier of subtitles, you will be introduced to so many more amazing films,” noted Bong Joon Ho as he collected the Foreign Language Film Golden Globe for Parasite (2019). A month later the Korean maestro was at the Academy Awards®, where he won the same prize. “The category has a new name now,” he said as he clutched his second Oscar® of the night. “[It’s gone from] Best Foreign Language Film to Best International Feature Film… I applaud and support the new direction that this change symbolizes.” “Foreign” is, of course, a matter of perspective, and the Academy’s aim was to reflect the diverse nature of the seventh art. This collection brings together some of the “amazing films” that have either won or been nominated for the Academy’s Best International Feature Film. Their dialogue may be spoken in myriad tongues, but the language of cinema is universal.

title: The More the Messier:
subtitle: Love Triangles on Film
full_title: The More the Messier: Love Triangles on Film
short_synopsis: The mathematics of attraction are far from a straightforward science. Between the sheets, two plus one can equal bliss—or a total catastrophe! Basking in the erotic unpredictability of three-way relationships, these alternative romances twist the conventions of cinema with sexiness and style.
long_synopsis: The mathematics of love are far from straightforward. Between the sheets, two plus one can equal ecstatic pleasure—or a total catastrophe! Resisting the norms of coupledom, the passionate paramours in this collection dare to indulge in the bliss of love triangles and ménages à trois, which come with emotional chaos as well as sexual liberation. Whether a long-term domestic setup or an erotic experiment, these alternative romances not only bend the rules of attraction but also twist cinematic conventions. Polyamory isn’t merely a playground for men, either, as seen in works from women filmmakers that foreground the explosiveness of female desire. Covering a wide range of eras and filmmaking styles, these sexy delights will inspire you to spice up your love life with style.

title: Guess Who?:
subtitle: Murder Mysteries
short_synopsis: Endlessly entertaining in their devilish twists and turns, murder mysteries thrill with their alluring settings and morbid intrigue, beckoning us to step into the role of the master detective. Time to grab your magnifying glass and keep your eyes peeled for the clues: can you guess who did it?
long_synopsis: Endlessly entertaining in their devilish twists and turns, the thrills of the murder mystery are pleasurably bountiful. They satisfy our infinite curiosity for foul play and conspiracy, beckoning us to step into the role of the master detective hot on the trail of a cunning suspect. Equally captivating are popular settings such as handsome mansions and exotic resorts where nothing and no-one are what they seem. More than just a cozy watch, the best murder mysteries hold a mirror to the hidden depths of human nature and class hierarchy. After all, few genres enjoy making wealthy characters suffer quite like the whodunnit! Whether committed in the throes of passion or premeditated with exquisite planning, a crime can unmask a trail of even more heinous deeds. Time to grab your magnifying glass and keep your eyes peeled for the clues: can you guess who did it?

title: Madcap Adventure:
subtitle: Comedies by Quentin Dupieux
full_title: Madcap Adventure: Comedies by Quentin Dupieux
short_synopsis: Plunging with glee into wilder-than-dreams weirdness, Quentin Dupieux is a cinematic surrealist for the 21st century. Up is down and left is right in these ludicrous comedies—and it all makes a strange sort of sense under the direction of Dupieux, who pulls off his hijinks with killer style.
long_synopsis: Quentin Dupieux is a cinematic surrealist for the 21st century. We know—surreal. It gets thrown about quite a bit, but the French filmmaker fits right into a dazzling European tradition of wilder-than-dreams weirdness that goes back to Salvador Dalí and Luis Buñuel. Concocting ludicrous modern equivalents to lobster telephones and sliced-up eyeballs in his perversely object-oriented adventures, Dupieux also cites Charlie Kaufman as an inspiration for how he metatextually twists his narratives inside out. Known to audiophiles as Mr. Oizo (a homophone for oiseau, or ‘bird’ in French), Dupieux was famous first of all as an electronic musician—although he had been excited about filmmaking since his teens. Cross-pollinating between the two métiers, he often shoots his own music videos and inserts his own computerized beats into his eccentric films. Rampant fun is the name of the game for this impish auteur, who delights in sending his characters on madcap schemes and stretching our imaginations to far-flung extremes. Who knows what strange rabbit hole we’ll find ourselves down with the jokester extraordinaire, but one thing is for sure: whatever Dupieux does, he always does it with killer style.

title: Soul Cinema:
subtitle: Films by Fatih Akin
full_title: Soul Cinema: Films by Fatih Akin
short_synopsis: Moving within and beyond Germany’s borders, Fatih Akin’s transnational output is poignantly grounded in memory, language, and music. Politically vital and cinematically enthralling, these films redefine our understanding of national cinema, painting an arresting picture of global interconnectedness.
long_synopsis: Moving within and beyond the borders of Germany, Fatih Akin’s cinema of lived experiences is poignantly grounded in memory, language, and music. Informed by his upbringing in Hamburg, the films of the prolific Turkish-German auteur are concerned in part with the shifting ideals of national identity. Emboldened by a sharp understanding of political frameworks, Akin’s films nimbly sidestep facile conclusions and maudlin sentimentality in favor of bold storytelling. Dynamically exploring recurring social issues, especially those impacting the Turkish diaspora in Germany and elsewhere, Akin’s oeuvre is remarkably varied in terms of genre and visual style. Credited with ushering in a new era of multicultural German filmmaking, Akin’s vital debut Short Sharp Shock (1998) is as tonally volatile as a classic Scorsese gangster film. Moving restlessly across the borders of Turkey and Germany, the critically acclaimed drama The Edge of Heaven (2007) is ambitious and expansive in its scope, weaving together interconnected storylines concerning family, loss, and redemption. Shot over the course of five years, the environmental documentary Polluting Paradise (2012) follows Akin to his grandparents’ home village, now turned into a garbage dump. Outside his own heritage, Akin flexes a different muscle in the riotous screwball comedy Soul Kitchen (2009), a charming look at a Greek tavern and a new kind of European multiculturalism. Equally compelling is the heart-stopping The Golden Glove (2019), a true-crime drama that sheds light on a series of gruesome murders in 1970s Hamburg. With The Cut (2014), which revisits the harrowing Armenian genocide during the first world war, the director tries his hand at the period drama to searing effect. Fusing the local and the global, the historical and the contemporary, the films of Fatih Akin redefine our understanding of national cinema, painting an arresting picture of global interconnectedness and care.

title: Hit the Road:
subtitle: Road Movies
short_synopsis: Out on the open road, the possibilities are truly endless. New horizons—and breathtaking landscapes—promise wild escapades, unexpected connections, and even introspection. No need to leave the comfort of your couch: these cinematic road trips are guaranteed to sate your thirst for adventure!
long_synopsis: Out on the open road, the possibilities are truly endless. A journey towards new horizons promises not only breathtaking sights, but also a chance for wild escapades, unexpected connections, and poignant introspection. Much can go wrong on a road trip, yet this quintessential potential for chaos is also what makes traveling so irresistible. Bumpy shenanigans aside, a change of view can also strengthen existing bonds, and even offer the possibility of healing. Sometimes moving forward also means looking inward. An invitation to forego the comforts of home—and the trappings of individuality—travel is not only a personal but communal act. While majestic landscapes offer a kind of communion between nature and man, chance encounters have the life-changing capacity to erase differences and foster new understandings. Such wondrously transformative epiphanies can be facilitated by the mere act of heading out to the highway. No need to leave the couch: these cinematic road trips are guaranteed to sate your thirst for adventure!

title: Millennial Meltdown
short_synopsis: Feeling crushed under the weight of adulthood? You are not alone! These restless, lovably messy souls are also navigating the rough tides of a quarter-life crisis and, simply, trying their best. No need to wait for permission to spiral: this is your invite to our millennial meltdown party.
long_synopsis: Feeling crushed under the existential weight of adulthood? You are not alone! The bewildered souls in our collection are also navigating the rough currents of a quarter-life crisis and are simply trying their best. In this age of colossal uncertainty, the pursuit of true love and a healthy work-life balance—or any kind of equilibrium at all—is more tumultuous than ever. Adrift between precarious jobs and even shakier relationships, these restless protagonists are almost too relatable in their messiness. A catalyst for exhilarating chaos, their reckless behavior makes for riotous comedy as well as high drama. As if a fickle dating pool and an exploitative job market are not enough of a headache, familial conundrums can also wreak havoc on the psyche. No need to wait for permission to spiral: this is your invite to our millennial meltdown party.

title: How to Be a Human:
subtitle: Films by Aki Kaurismäki
full_title: How to Be a Human: Films by Aki Kaurismäki
short_synopsis: Few filmmakers boast a style as inimitable as the great Finnish auteur, Aki Kaurismäki. Moving to the insistent beat of rock ‘n’ roll, and illuminated by the timeless lighting and color palettes of classical Hollywood, Kaurismäki’s films are, above all, masterclasses in how to be a human being.
long_synopsis: Few filmmakers boast a style as inimitable as Aki Kaurismäki. His is a distinctly humanist cinema, one suffused with empathy for the common man. Bohemian, ruthlessly unsentimental, and tinged with a dark sense of humor, the world of Kaurismäki is one in which ordinary people rub up against a system designed to demoralize them—a world in which the surreal offers a path out of the hardships of the everyday. As the most recent winner of the Jury Prize at the Cannes Film Festival for his achingly beautiful Fallen Leaves (2023), there’s no disputing that the great iconoclast is the leading voice in Finnish cinema. With its vibrant color schemes, meticulous camera moves, and rigorous mise-en-scène, Kaurismäki’s work is instantly recognizable. Sometimes gut-bustingly funny, sometimes suffused with straight-faced drollery, his films are ever-ready to counter a character’s existential dread with a fanciful leap into the absurd. Moving to the insistent beat of rock ‘n’ roll, and illuminated by the timeless lighting and color palettes of classical Hollywood, the films in this career-spanning collection are, above all, masterclasses in how to be a human being.

title: Neo-westerns:
subtitle: A New Frontier
short_synopsis: A genre as old as cinema itself, the western has gone through countless cycles of fascinating reinvention. Celebrating the release of Pedro Almodóvar’s Strange Way of Life, a queer ode to frontier life, our neo-western collection gallops towards new horizons. Saddle up, partner, and enjoy the ride!
long_synopsis: A genre as old as cinema itself, the western—and its classic tension between social order and the immense wilderness—has gone through countless cycles of fascinating reinvention. Celebrating the release of Pedro Almodóvar’s Strange Way of Life (2023), which imbues the crimson desert with sizzling queer passions, our collection of neo-westerns gallops toward a new cinematic frontier. Examining the legacy of the Oregon Trail during 19th-century America, Kelly Reichardt’s First Cow (2019) tempers the racial violence of the time with a poignant tale of friendship between a Jewish cook and a Chinese migrant, two tender souls who strive for survival amid a vast and unforgiving landscape. Moving beyond the borders of the United States, the transnational neo-westerns in this collection filter familiar conventions through a distinctively local lens. While Valeska Grisebach’s volatile Western (2017) conjures a fractured vision of Europeanness, Kim Jee-woon’s smash hit The Good, the Bad, the Weird (2008) transports the gun-toting thrills of the spaghetti western—right down to the guitar twangs—to 1940s Manchuria. Set in South Africa and Brazil respectively, Kleber Mendonça Filho and Juliano Dornelles’s Bacurau (2019) and Jenna Cato Bass’s Flatland (2019) powerfully resist the common perception of the western as a conservative, male-dominated genre. Here, the domain of the outlaw is charged with feminist and revolutionary possibility. Grappling with ongoing sociopolitical issues, these exciting revivals of the western reaffirm the evergreen potency of a beloved genre as they look toward new challenges on the horizon. Saddle up, partner, and enjoy the ride.

title: Adèle Exarchopoulos:
subtitle: Fire Starter
short_synopsis: Fiercely talented, award-winning actor Adèle Exarchopoulos is a dynamite presence in contemporary cinema. Burning bright since an early age, the Parisienne’s star shows no signs of fading: ever the fearless and passionate performer, she is brilliant and she is inextinguishable.
long_synopsis: Adèle Exarchopoulos is a dynamite presence in contemporary cinema. Burning bright since an early age, the actor’s star shows no signs of fading. After she started acting at thirteen, the precocious Parisienne went on to become the youngest winner ever of the Palme d’Or, in an incendiary moment in Cannes history. She continued to perform with fearless passion in the films that followed, drawing on deep wells of emotion to round out strikingly raw physicality. Fiercely talented, Exarchopoulos acts with such intensity that our screens start to feel flimsy when she appears: as if the line between fantasy and reality could, exhilaratingly, catch light at any moment.

title: International Booker Prize 2023
short_synopsis: Since 2005, the International Booker Prize has celebrated the best fiction translated into English from other languages. With the winning title of the 2023 edition due to be announced on May 23, we’re thrilled to present a series of films to complement each title on this year’s shortlist.
long_synopsis: Since 2005, the International Booker Prize has celebrated the best fiction translated into English from other languages. With the winning title of the 2023 edition due to be announced on May 23, MUBI has teamed up with the esteemed literary award to present a series of films which serve to complement each title on this year’s shortlist. As the International Booker Prize team said recently: “We believe there is a natural symbiosis between international fiction and international film, and between readers and viewers of both. We therefore want to encourage people who love translated fiction to explore more global cinema, and vice versa, expanding their cultural horizons.” With each film reflecting the themes or tone of its paired book, our series offers a range of delights for cinephiles and bookworms alike.

title: To Sleep So as to Dream:
subtitle: Films by Apichatpong Weerasethakul
full_title: To Sleep So as to Dream:  Films by Apichatpong Weerasethakul
short_synopsis: To watch the films of Apichatpong Weerasethakul is to be set adrift through liminal, transitional spaces, where spiritual borders are as permeable as those between consciousness and sleep. Our series is an invitation to a cinematic landscape delicately hewn from such stuff as dreams are made on.
long_synopsis: It’s fitting that Apichatpong Weerasethakul’s Palme d’Or winner Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives (2010) should open in the twilight hours: a time between night and day, between wakefulness and sleep. The cinema of the great Thai filmmaker haunts such liminal, transitional spaces, where spiritual borders are as permeable as those between consciousness and dreams. It’s a fascination made literal in Cemetery of Splendour (2015), in which ancient spirits cross into the modern world at will, and a group of soldiers are struck down by a mysterious sleeping sickness. For Apichatpong, the relationship between cinemagoing and the dream landscapes of the unconscious is symbiotic. “I always believe that we possess the best cinema,” he said. “When we sleep, it’s our own image and experience that we edit at night and process.” For his first feature made outside his native Thailand, Apichatpong Weerasethakul ventured to Colombia with the great Tilda Swinton for Memoria (2021). Winner of the Jury Prize at Cannes, the film is a dazzling metaphysical mystery that reverberates with the weight of history, memory, and identity. A cinematic experiment in shooting with a low frame-rate, the short film Ashes (2012) illuminates, in flashes of stuttering radiance, instances of daily life in the Thai countryside, from the mundane to the surprisingly sublime. Occupied with reincarnation, physical transcendence, and porous borders both literal and spiritual, the oneiric films in our series are an invitation to a cinematic world delicately hewn from such stuff as dreams are made on.

title: Short Films
subtitle: Big Names
short_synopsis: Featuring essential and thrilling shorts crafted by some of the best directors working today, this special focus proves that size does not matter. More than mere interludes, these little treasures from beloved titans provide a crucial window into the formation and evolution of a signature style.
long_synopsis: Just as essential and thrilling as their more famous feature-length siblings, these unmissable shorts from some of the best directors working today prove that size does not matter. From early-career experiments to surprising detours from established names, our curated selection offers a crucial window into both the formation and the evolution of a signature style. Sheer perfection in small doses, these little treasures from beloved titans are ready for their close-ups.

title: British Cinema
short_synopsis: This evolving collection of films seeks to spotlight some of the most innovative British cinematic voices and singular artistic perspectives that the UK has to offer.
long_synopsis: Cinema can open a window on a nation’s culture, revealing the many ways we are different, and what we have in common. Films can speak to the specifics of the place from which they emerge: bringing to life the melting pot of cultural traditions, languages, and politics. This evolving collection of films seeks to spotlight some of the most innovative cinematic voices and singular artistic perspectives that the UK has to offer.

title: Lights, Camera... Action!
short_synopsis: It’s hard to beat the rush of adrenaline that comes with a well-staged action sequence. From punch-ups and shoot-outs to thrilling chases and jaw-dropping stunts, the films in this series are fast on their feet; explosive tales that put the human body—and a filmmaker’s mettle—to the test.
long_synopsis: It’s hard to beat the rush of adrenaline that comes with a well-staged action sequence. From punch-ups and shoot-outs to thrilling chases and jaw-dropping stunts, the action movie is designed to set pulses racing. Drawing on the formal properties unique to cinema, it’s little wonder that the action film is as old as the medium itself. Technological advancements may have shattered the limits of possibility for what a set-piece can achieve, but its intentions remain the same: pure cinematic spectacle. Action is no slave to genre, and knows nothing of international boundaries. It can dominate a picture, or erupt in the unlikeliest of moments, turbo-charging a story with a sudden jolt of electricity. The films in this series are fast on their feet; explosive tales that put the human body—and a filmmaker’s mettle—to the test. At its best, little can touch an action sequence as a showcase for awe-inspiring directorial virtuosity.

title: Family Viewing
short_synopsis: It is never too early to become a movie lover, and our collection of family-friendly films promises a wonderful bonding time for youngsters and adults alike. From colorful treasures of cinema history to modern classics, come with MUBI and step into a world of pure imagination!
long_synopsis: Few activities can bring the whole family together quite like a fun movie night. It is never too early to become a movie lover, and our collection of family-friendly films promises a wonderful bonding time for youngsters and adults alike. From colorful treasures of cinema history to modern classics, come with MUBI and step into a world of pure imagination!

title: The Souvenir:
subtitle: A Joanna Hogg Diptych
full_title: The Souvenir: A Joanna Hogg Diptych
short_synopsis: A profound exploration of love, grief, and the toils of the creative process, Joanna Hogg’s luminous The Souvenir: Part I & II diptych dazzles with its uncanny intimacy. Like the eponymous Fragonard painting, these films are one-of-a-kind masterpieces that hold us captive and endlessly inspire.
long_synopsis: In The Souvenir (2019) and The Souvenir: Part II (2021), the dazzling cinematic diptych from British master Joanna Hogg, the entirety of a relationship can be poignantly felt in a plethora of objects. Named after a Jean-Honoré Fragonard painting currently hung at the Wallace Collection in London, these delicate tableaux of a young artist grappling with a tortured lover while coming into her own voice find the profound in the domestic: an overcoat or a French brass bed–Hogg’s own–can ache with passions as well as heartbreak. Such emotional and stylistic precision, along with a blend of the theatrical and the autobiographical, bring an uncanny intimacy to Hogg’s depiction of British upper class milieu and the toils of film school. While the casting of early-career collaborator Tilda Swinton and her daughter, the compelling Honor Swinton Byrne, brings an extratextual layer of magnetism to The Souvenir, its sublime second chapter magnificently encapsulates the height of Hogg’s artistic prowess through a brilliant, meta-exploration of grief and artmaking catharses. Soul-stirring, luminous, and formally ambitious, these films carry a spellbinding beauty that rivals the enigma of Fragonard’s painting; they too are one-of-a-kind masterpieces that hold us captive, a source of inspiration for years to come.

title: The One and Only:
subtitle: Tilda Swinton
full_title: The One and Only: Tilda Swinton
short_synopsis: Effacing the borders of gender and genre, the chameleonic Tilda Swinton embodies the infinite possibilities of cinema. Charting the electric breadth of her screen personas, our retrospective celebrates a multi-hyphenated artist who has inspired us all to step out of the box and reach for the stars.
long_synopsis: Effacing the borders of gender and genre, the chameleonic Tilda Swinton embodies the infinite possibilities of cinema. One of the greatest performers of our time, Swinton possesses the kind of striking, androgynous features that close-ups adore, yet she can also disappear into her roles with extraordinary ease. With an ability to shape-shift between regality, ferocity, and muted passion, Swinton’s face is the perfect canvas for filmmakers to conjure the depths of their artistic obsessions. In this sense, she is not only a part of the artwork, but a human work of art herself. Drawn toward different modes of visual and storytelling experimentation, Swinton has been an integral presence in arthouse film, collaborating with an extraordinary cohort of filmmakers from across the globe. Charting the electric breadth of Swinton’s on-screen personas, our retrospective celebrates a multi-hyphenated artist who inspires us all to step out of the box and reach for the stars.

title: Joachim Trier:
subtitle: The Oslo Trilogy
short_synopsis: Spanning over fifteen years, Joachim Trier’s “Oslo” trilogy observes the unceasing march of time and modernity. Documenting a city in flux, these mercurial character portraits embrace the uncertainties and melancholy of adulthood with striking pathos.
long_synopsis: Spanning over fifteen years, Joachim Trier’s “Oslo” trilogy observes the unceasing tides of time and modernity. The first film in this accidental trilogy, Reprise (2006) is a visceral and punk-inflected look at the friendship between two young men who aspire to be important writers. Tinged with a bruised melancholy, the masterful existential drama Oslo, August 31st (2011) chronicles 24 hours in the life of a burned-out recovering drug addict as he reels and cavorts his way through the city whilst on an overnight trip from rehab. This loose literary adaptation of Pierre Drieu La Rochelle’s 1931 novel Will O’ the Wisp also stars one of Trier’s favorite actors (and a superstar doctor), Anders Danielsen Lie, the trilogy’s star, who reappears as the faded comic book artist and love interest of Julie in The Worst Person in the World. Already adored,  Trier’s international hit expands on the questions of personal and artistic purpose and self-doubt that permeate the trilogy. Here, though, we see through the eyes of a twenty-something urban millennial woman, played with free-spirited abandon by the award-winning actor Renate Reinsve. The three films in Trier’s trilogy are as much documents of a city in flux as they are portraits of people questioning their role in life and what place self-expression might play in it. The unstoppable onslaught of  time, another common theme, is also experienced by viewers through its impact on the changing face of a modernizing Oslo throughout the last 15 years of the trilogy’s making. Trier’s moving, melancholic films reflect on what it means to fulfill or to fail to live up to one’s promise. Meanwhile, time marches on.

title: Featured on the MUBI Podcast
short_synopsis: Every episode of the MUBI Podcast is a thrilling journey of discovery through the work of some of the world’s greatest filmmakers. Featuring films mentioned on the show, this dazzling collection of global cinematic treasures is the perfect accompaniment to the cultural histories unearthed each week.
long_synopsis: From deep dives through the hidden backwaters of film history, to up-to-the-minute interviews with some of the biggest names in international cinema, every episode of the MUBI Podcast is a journey of discovery. Bringing together some of the films featured on the show, this collection of cinematic treasures from around the world is the perfect accompaniment to the fascinating cultural histories unearthed each week.

title: The Darkroom Dreams of Peter Tscherkassky
full_title: The Darkroom Dreams of Peter Tscherkassky
short_synopsis: To celebrate Train Again, we’re bringing together a series of Peter Tscherkassky’s analogue experiments in the darkroom. A major figure in contemporary found-footage filmmaking, the Austrian avant-garde artist is devoted to the sensory potential of celluloid, and committed to unveiling its artifice.
long_synopsis: Here at MUBI we adore the work of the found-footage maverick Peter Tscherkassky: so much so that we asked him, in 2019, to dream up an interpretation of our logo to show before some of our films. In typical Tscherkassky style, it arrived in a whirr of flickering film strips and the industrial sounds of trains and projection—a feat created by using clips of the Lumiére brothers’ work. This year, we’re immensely pleased to be hosting the latest film from the Austrian master of the avant-garde on the platform. In a nod to the fellow Austrian avant-garde filmmaker Kurt Kren,  Train Again mounts a frenetic, flash-framed tribute to early cinema’s love affair with trains. To celebrate Train Again, we’re bringing together a series of Tscherkassky’s analogue experiments in the darkroom. A major figure in contemporary found-footage filmmaking, the filmmaker is both devoted to the sensory and tactile potential of celluloid, and committed to unveiling its artifice. Manually reworking pre-existing film—an arduous analog process— Tscherkassky draws out oneiric imagery from the scraps and glitches of film history. Inviting us to reflect on how we consume cinematic images of all sorts—from horror films to westerns to pornographic films—Tscherkassky’s films aim to deconstruct cinema’s many motifs and narrative norms. Dive into Tscherkassky’s exquisite corpus of films, for a cinema that is both alluringly cerebral and thrillingly visceral.

title: Franz Rogowski:
subtitle: The Man of the Hour
full_title: Franz Rogowski: The Man of the Hour
short_synopsis: A beguiling screen presence of stunning intensity, Franz Rogowski has taken the international film world by storm. Celebrating his latest triumph in Great Freedom, our spotlight raises a glass to the man of the hour, and one of the most exciting actors currently gracing the silver screen.
long_synopsis: A beguiling screen presence of both physical and emotional intensity, German actor Franz Rogowski has taken the international film world by storm. From Christian Petzold to Terrence Malick, Rogowski has worked with an astonishing array of formidable directors, exhibiting a striking versatility as a performer across a delightfully unpredictable multitude of genres. Initially trained as a dancer and a choreographer, Rogowski brings a rare attention to the physicality of his characters, whose dialogues as well as bodily gestures bear equal poignancy. Such a gift is already apparent in an early-career performance in Jakob Lass’s improvisational Love Steaks (2013) where Rogowski plays a young spa trainee whose awkward bedside manners sparkle with an irresistible charm. With his strong brows and soulful eyes, Rogowski carries the kind of visage that is at once distinctively cinematic yet increasingly elusive among the recent crop of young actors. The same reservedness that endears in the unlikely romance of Thomas Stuber’s In the Aisles (2018) can also morph into a more frightening expression of childlike wrath in Peter Brunner’s Luzifer (2021).
