Thus, to behold a Rogowski performance is to pleasantly prepare oneself for the unexpected. Rogowski’s stunning ability to oscillate between romantic vulnerability and quiet rage culminates in his latest triumph in Sebastian Meise’s Great Freedom (2021), a searing historical account of the social and legal cruelty suffered by gay men in post-war Germany. To celebrate this mesmerizing turn from Rogowski, our spotlight raises a glass to the man of the hour, and one of the most exciting actors currently gracing the silver screen.

title: Drawn to Life:
subtitle: Animated Films
short_synopsis: From startling stop-motion to mechanical innovation, the tactility of hand-drawn images to dazzling CGI: animation’s possibilities are endless. This series brings together animated gems from around the globe, all of them testament to this most profoundly liberating and shape-shifting medium.
long_synopsis: From startling stop-motion to mechanical innovation, the tactility of hand-drawn images to dazzling CGI: animation’s possibilities are endless. Just about any substance you can think of has been brought into the frame to create the marvelous illusion of movement, from clay to cutouts, puppets to putty, robots, sand, and string. Dating back to the birth of cinema, animated films have captivated audiences with their ability to breathe life into what was previously thought impossible, visualizing our wildest dreams, hopes, and fears. Delighting in metamorphosis, animation is distinctive for its ability to shift restlessly from one image to another through manipulating cel or substance. This series brings together animated gems from around the globe, all of them testament to this most profoundly liberating and shape-shifting medium.

title: Cut to Black:
subtitle: Celebrating Black Cinema
full_title: Cut to Black: Celebrating Black Cinema
short_synopsis: Cut to Black celebrates the incredible wealth of Black artistry, both in front of and behind the camera. From acclaimed classics to hidden treasures, these distinctive achievements in film look to the past, reflect the present, and gesture to the future of cinema.
long_synopsis: A celebration of Black talent in cinema, both in front of and behind the camera, the titles in this series bear witness to the incredible wealth of Black artistry, vibrantly present in an eclectic range of works. From acclaimed classics to hidden treasures, social critiques to genre pleasures, these films powerfully articulate the struggles, resilience, and joy experienced by the Black community all around the world. They speak to the lived realities and the significance of representation in the arts. Honoring the inimitable artists who have enriched our screens, this selection of distinctive achievements in film looks to the past, reflects the present, and gestures to the future of cinema.

title: Festival Focus:
subtitle: Venice
short_synopsis: For more than 90 years, filmmakers have convened on the Lido of the Venice lagoon every September for the best in international cinema. We present a series of spellbinding discoveries and major films from some of cinema’s biggest names, all from past editions of the world’s oldest film festival.
long_synopsis: For more than 90 years, stars and filmmakers have convened on the Lido of the Venice lagoon every September for a celebration of the best in international cinema. Wind your way with us through the canals and over the Bridge of Sighs as we present a series of spellbinding discoveries and major films from some of cinema’s biggest names, all from past editions of the world’s oldest film festival.

title: Latest & Greatest
short_synopsis: Showcasing some of the most inspiring and compelling new films, fresh from cinemas and festivals. A world of bold new discoveries awaits you, exclusively on MUBI.
long_synopsis: Showcasing some of the most inspiring and compelling new films, fresh from cinemas and festivals. A world of bold new discoveries awaits you, exclusively on MUBI.

title: Role Reversals:
subtitle: When Actors Direct
short_synopsis: From Harry Langdon to Ida Lupino, Michel Piccoli to Greta Gerwig, actors have ventured into the director’s chair since the early days of cinema. This series looks at actors-turned-directors—old and new—and the surprises that arise in switching from one side of the camera to the other.
long_synopsis: From Harry Langdon to Ida Lupino, Michel Piccoli to Greta Gerwig, actors have ventured into the director’s chair since the early days of cinema. For some, it’s the prospect of gaining greater creative freedom that appeals, for others it’s a personal attachment to a certain story, or the urge to put into practice a directorial vision first grasped on set. Some actors find a whole new career-path opening up, while others walk away with a single, one-off credit. This series looks at actors-turned-directors—old and new—and the surprises that arise in switching from one side of the camera to the other.

title: Golden Palms, Lions, and Bears, Oh My!:
subtitle: Award-winning Greats
full_title: Golden Palms, Lions, and Bears, Oh My!: Award-winning Greats
short_synopsis: An integral part of cinema history, movie awards have at once shaped film canons and amplified otherwise overlooked works. Skipping the red carpets and hours-long ceremonies, our selection of award-winning films will take you right to a shining treasure trove of garlanded cinema.
long_synopsis: Just like darkened theater halls and soft red seats, glittering awards—and the visceral reactions they evoke—have been an integral part of cinema history. Golden statues, though sometimes cheekily used by winners as doorstops, not only shape film canons but also contribute to the medium’s allure. Over the years, academies and festivals have crowned epic cinematic undertakings as well as uplifting and introducing smaller productions to a wider audience, all while celebrating otherwise overlooked aspects of filmmaking. Skipping the red carpets and hours-long ceremonies, our selection of award-winning films will take you right to a shining treasure trove of garlanded cinema.

title: Embracing Infamy:
subtitle: Cult Films
short_synopsis: Presenting a collection of the weird and the wonderful. These are the pictures that were cast adrift on the mainstream cinematic highways and passed between fellow off-road travellers with a knowing smile, the genre oddities that clawed their way to the top. Welcome to the cult.
long_synopsis: Welcome to the weird, wonderful and irreverent world of the cult film. These are the cinematic outsiders, cast adrift from the mainstream highways and passed between fellow off-road travelers with a knowing smile. Cult films don’t always start out being loved. They need time to find their kin. Some are victims of neglectful parentage—kicked to the curb by delinquent distributors and ignored by the masses to desultory box office returns, only finding favor in their theatrical afterlife. Others played to enthusiastic audiences who passionately championed their nonconformist allure where critics and marketing departments had failed. Cult films have little time for the boundaries of decorum or taste, and are invariably defined by their resistance to definition. One thing is for certain: you can’t make a cult film by design. Their reputation can only be made—or defined—by the communities that rally around them, thriving by virtue of their charismatic, transgressive charms. These are the genre oddities that clawed their way to the top, word-of-mouth success stories from the cinematic school of hard knocks. They’re the films that fought for their place in the canon, even when the odds were stacked against them.

title: Growing Pains:
subtitle: Coming-of-age Films
short_synopsis: Charting the journey from childhood into adulthood, the coming-of-age film is one of spiritual and moral awakening. These are tales of resilience and hope, of growing pains and teenage rebellion, every one of them a testament to the universal truth that growing up never comes easy.
long_synopsis: Charting the journey from childhood into adulthood, the coming-of-age film is an infinitely malleable genre marked by emotional upheaval. Whether sparked by trauma, desire, or simply change itself, these invariably character-driven films, in all their permutations, remain defined by the spiritual or moral growth of a protagonist making it through to the other side. These are tales of awakenings brought on by external forces, defining events that force a confrontation with the realities and responsibilities of adulthood, often ahead of time. They are stories of resilience and hope, of growing pains and teenage rebellion, every one of them a testament to the universal truth that growing up never comes easy.

title: Phantoms Among Us:
subtitle: The Films of Christian Petzold
full_title: Phantoms Among Us: The Films of Christian Petzold
short_synopsis: Presenting the genre riffs and spectral, in-between landscapes of Christian Petzold’s psychological thrillers. Our retrospective showcases some of his greatest films, a collective vision of Germany’s past, present, and future unlike any other in modern cinema.
long_synopsis: There may not be any ghosts, literally speaking, in the films of Christian Petzold, but that doesn’t mean they’re not haunted in other ways. The most prominent figure to come out of the so-called “Berlin School”—an aesthetically-driven, realist mode of political cinema that emerged in the 1990s—Petzold takes the spectral, indeterminate landscapes of post-wall, late-capitalist Germany and infuses them with a deep love for the pleasures of genre filmmaking. His protagonists are invariably in motion, unwilling transients in a ghostland of in-between spaces and places. These determined, fiercely autonomous characters ricochet between socio-political borders, both visible and invisible, while tethered to the fatalistic mandates of Petzold’s genre constructs. From The Postman Always Rings Twice and Carnival of Souls to Vertigo, Petzold reconditions genre classics with clinical precision. His thrillers are not straightforward remakes by any means, but rather variants on a theme, reupholstering iconic antecedents as psychologically acute and culturally-specific explorations of empowerment within hostile systems. Whether examining contemporary concerns or exhuming the nation’s past with his masterful period pieces, Petzold finds his homeland in a permanent state of flux. Our retrospective presents some of the greatest films from his thirty-year career, a collective vision of Germany’s past, present and future unlike any other in modern cinema.

title: Pride Unprejudiced:
subtitle: LGBTQ+ Cinema
short_synopsis: From intimate dramas to daring experimental features, our collection of LGBTQ+ cinema is a celebration of queer artistry and history, highlighting urgent issues and celebrating the sheer pleasure of unapologetic queer joy.
long_synopsis: Stepping out of the celluloid closet, LGBTQ+ representations in cinema have become increasingly robust, diverse, and multidimensional. From intimate character studies to boldly experimental features, the eclecticism of queer cinema not only illuminates the richness of our collective human experience but also daringly subverts filmmaking conventions. Just like the delightfully broad spectrum of genders and sexualities, cinematic renderings of queer experiences are cultural mosaics that rise above attempts at classification and categorization. Reflecting this formal dynamism, our electric offering of LGBTQ+ cinema is a celebration of queer artistry and history, spotlighting urgent social issues and the sheer pleasure of unapologetic queer joy.

title: 25 Years of the
subtitle: Human Rights Watch Film Festival
full_title: 25 Years of the Human Rights Watch Film Festival
short_synopsis: To celebrate the 25th edition of Human Rights Watch Film Festival in London, running from the 18-26 March, we are proud to present a selection of festival highlights from previous editions.
long_synopsis: To celebrate the 25th edition of Human Rights Watch Film Festival in London, running from 18-26 March, we are proud to present a selection of festival highlights from previous editions, in an ongoing partnership with one of the world’s leading independent organizations dedicated to defending and protecting human rights.

title: In the Mood
subtitle: for Love
short_synopsis: Get ready to fall in love with some of our favorite cinematic romances. Whether it’s the thrill of that first encounter or the depth of a decade-long affair, a quick tryst or a bitter breakup, love can take many forms. Here are some of the best swoon-worthy romances to warm the coldest of hearts.
long_synopsis: Get ready to fall in love with some of our favorite cinematic romances. Whether it’s the thrill of that first encounter or the depth of a decade-long affair, a quick and dramatic tryst or the bitter regret of a breakup, love can take many shapes and forms, inducing all manner of emotions. Here are some of the best swoon-worthy romances to warm the coldest of hearts.

title: Festival Focus:
subtitle: Locarno Film Festival
short_synopsis: Taking place in a scenic lakeside Swiss town every August, the Locarno Film Festival is home to the grandmasters as well as the emerging stars of tomorrow. MUBI exclusively presents a series of highlights from one of the world’s premiere venues for celebrating bold, adventurous cinema.
long_synopsis: “The future begins now, and Locarno will always be on the side of cinema.” Home to the grandmasters as well as the emerging stars of tomorrow, the Locarno Film Festival takes place in a scenic lakeside Swiss town every August. MUBI exclusively presents a series of highlights from this brilliant festival, one of the world’s premiere venues for celebrating bold, adventurous cinema.

title: Fear on Film
short_synopsis: Like a sneaky ghost on a moonless night, Halloween season is already upon us. For this spookiest time of the year, plunge into the heart of darkness with this spine-chilling selection of cinematic horrors. Watch if you dare!
long_synopsis: Like a sneaky ghost on a moonless night, Halloween season is already upon us. From the earliest days of cinema, audiences have been drawn to scary movies like moths to a flame. Horror films, beyond the creeping pleasures of gore and the shivery shocks of jump scares, allure by offering an imaginary realm in which we can face our otherwise hidden anxieties and nightmares. Many of the most famous stories in horror cinema, and in gothic literature before it, tap into mythic narratives that appeal to our base emotions. Whether exploring good versus evil, our fundamental need for safety, or the visceral thrills of suspense and discomfort, these films probe the nooks and crannies of the human subconscious. Beyond finding therapy in horror films, enjoying them can be a collective ritual. As we come together in this season of fear to enjoy its many thrills, this collection plunges us into the heart of darkness with a selection of striking cinematic horror. Watch if you dare!

title: Portrait of the Artist
short_synopsis: A series of portraits of the most influential artists of our time, whose work spans a wide range of crafts and practices. These films provide an alluring window into the creative process of these inspiring figures, who have changed our viewpoints on the world in a myriad of enchanting ways.
long_synopsis: A series of portraits of the most influential artists of our time, whose work spans a wide range of crafts and practices. These films provide an alluring window into the creative process of these inspiring figures, who have changed our viewpoints on the world in a myriad of enchanting ways.

title: Shorts
short_synopsis: Throughout cinema history, short filmmaking has been at the vanguard of formal and storytelling experimentation, providing a creative haven for budding and seasoned directors alike. Compact in length and rich in visual innovation, these eclectic gems in our collection show that less is always more.
long_synopsis: While often eclipsed by full-length features, short films have continuously been at the vanguard of formal and storytelling inventions. After all, cinema itself began with mere seconds-long sequences of moving images! Not only can they reveal unexpected aspects of a seasoned filmmaker’s oeuvre, these bite-sized works also look ahead to the future stars that would soon grace our cinematic horizon. For experimental and marginalized artists who find themselves outside of the hierarchical mainstream, short filmmaking has additionally proved to be a haven for creative freedom and flexibility. Compact in running time and rich in visual innovation, the eclectic treasures in our collection of short films show that less is always more.

title: Documentaries
short_synopsis: Think talking heads and archive footage, or imagine flies on walls: What nonfiction cinema encompasses changes with each decade. Whether being creative with truth or observing life as it is, documentaries describe the world, spinning on the sharp axis of reality.
long_synopsis: What nonfiction cinema encompasses changes with each decade. In cinema’s earliest moments, silver screens flickered with Lumière actualities, city symphonies, and expeditions to wondrous terrains. As the twentieth century continued, wars and other revolutions were memorialized in inventive reenactments and arresting cinematic testimonies. More intimate diary and essay films—often characterized by poetic, playful approaches to reality—flourished with the rise of affordable equipment, while later advancements in high-definition cameras made some nature documentaries more spectacular than ever before. Observational modes, hybrid forms, and archival remixes might count among the most recent nonfiction trends, yet whatever methods used, these films invariably spin on the sharp axis of reality. Ultimately, documentaries describe the world.

title: Funny Ha Ha
short_synopsis: Everyone could do with a good laugh. From slapstick and screwball to arthouse subversion and sharp political satire, this collection contains everything you need to soothe the soul.
long_synopsis: Everyone could do with a good laugh. From slapstick and screwball to arthouse subversion and sharp political satire—all by way of a perfectly timed sight gag or impromptu, unexpected moment: the cinema has a long history of making people laugh, easing their troubles, and poking fun at those in power.

title: Reframing:
subtitle: Women Directors
short_synopsis: This collection showcases work by women auteurs spanning a diverse array of themes, styles, and genres. It’s time to reframe the conversation and focus our lens on films outside of the male-dominated canon of cinema.
long_synopsis: For as long as the movies have existed, women have been making them. This goes right back to the birth of the industry, when Alice Guy-Blaché, coming up through the studio of Léon Gaumont, directed one of the first narrative films, The Cabbage Fairy, back in 1896. And it runs up to the present moment, when increasing numbers of women directors are breaking through in an industry that for too long has held them back. This collection showcases work by women auteurs spanning a diverse array of themes, styles, and genres. It’s time to reframe the conversation and focus our lens on films outside of the male-dominated canon of cinema.

title: Films about Films
short_synopsis: Like the best of magicians, cinema delights in hiding its tricks. More than love letters to an art form, the films in this collection turn their gaze towards the filmmaking process itself, capturing the unique power that moving images continue to hold over countless cinema aficionados.
long_synopsis: Like the best of magicians, cinema delights in hiding its tricks. The films in this collection, however, let us in on the secrets of the trade. More than love letters to an art form, they make visible the unseen techniques, the production conditions, as well as the tribulations that lie behind the smoke and mirrors of movie wizardry. From engrossing dramas to rigorous documentaries, films about filmmaking dive deep into the mysteries of the creative process, capturing the unique power that moving images continue to hold over countless cinema aficionados.

title: Festival Focus:
subtitle: Berlinale
short_synopsis: Widely celebrated for showcasing socially and politically engaged cinema, the Berlinale is one of the highlights of the global film festival calendar. MUBI is rolling out the red carpet with a series of diverse, independent, and daring highlights from past editions of the festival.
long_synopsis: Widely celebrated for showcasing socially and politically engaged cinema, the Berlin International Film Festival (or Berlinale) remains one of the major highlights of the global film festival calendar. First inaugurated in 1951, a lot has changed from its unique Cold War origins at the heart of a divided city. In partnership with Berlinale, MUBI is rolling out the red carpet for a series of diverse, independent, and daring highlights from past editions of the festival.

title: Turn It Up:
subtitle: Music on Film
full_title: Turn It Up: Music on Film
short_synopsis: The adrenaline-pumping vibrations that musical performances elicit are at once physical and spiritual. Encompassing both documentary and fiction, our music-on-film picks not only celebrate the electric magnetism of live concerts but also explore the trials and tribunals of the artists themselves.
long_synopsis: Nothing can quite match the visceral vibrations of a live concert. The excitement of buying a ticket, the heart-palpitating wait before the show, the spontaneous camaraderie with friends both new and old, that coveted moment when a favorite song flows straight from the stage to envelop your entire body. All of these contribute to an other-worldly experience that is at once physical and spiritual. Encompassing both documentary and fiction, our selection of music films celebrates the electric magnetism of performance, but also moves beyond the glitz and glamor of the stage to dive into the lives, trials, and tribunals of the artists themselves.

title: Double Act:
subtitle: Nina Hoss & Christian Petzold
full_title: Double Act:  Nina Hoss & Christian Petzold
short_synopsis: The collaboration of Christian Petzold and Nina Hoss has been an alchemical pairing with few worthy rivals in contemporary cinema. Two of their finest films together are Barbara and Phoenix: sociological melodramas set at particularly fraught points in Germany’s history during the 20th century.
long_synopsis: Speaking at a career talk during the London Film Festival in 2020, German director and screenwriter Christian Petzold said, “After the first three or four days of shooting I have the feeling I have found someone. I’ve found someone who I’ve never searched for before.” He was speaking of his leading actress and frequent collaborator, Nina Hoss, and how she brings her characters to life in a revelatory fashion. To date, their six-film collaboration has been an alchemical pairing with few worthy rivals in contemporary cinema. Barbara (2012) and Phoenix (2014) count among Petzold and Hoss’s finest and most heartbreaking films. Each is a sociological melodrama set at particularly fraught points in Germany’s history during the 20th century. Barbara takes place in 1980s East Germany, where a Berlin doctor who has been forcibly moved to a rural medical clinic suspects Stasi informants are watching her every move. Phoenix, meanwhile, sees a German-Jewish survivor of Auschwitz return home unrecognizable: she has experienced horrors, and had to undergo reconstructive surgery too. Both films tell of a doomed relationship between a woman and a man (played on both occasions by Ronald Zehrfeld), driven by circumstances where no one can say what they really mean, or be clear about how much they really know about another person. Hoss’s face is crucial across the two films, with each gesture and glance delivering a weight that words cannot convey. Creating portraits of paranoia filled with suspense, Petzold and Hoss are in perfect sync in these remarkable, spellbinding films.

title: Juliette Binoche:
subtitle: The Woman With a Thousand Faces
full_title: Juliette Binoche: The Woman With a Thousand Faces
short_synopsis: Our screens would look different (duller! worse!) without four decades of the incomparable Juliette Binoche, whose versatility scintillates in some of contemporary cinema’s finest moments. From comedy to tragedy, she is a woman of a thousand faces—and a face that launched a thousand film lovers too.
long_synopsis: “I don’t get top billing, but that will come.” One of the most famous faces in international cinema, Juliette Binoche was a precocious talent when she came to film in the mid-1980s. Saying that self-confident line in her breakout role, the Parisienne quickly won the attention of many, in France and beyond. Gamine when she first came to the screen, Binoche flickered with versatility immediately: that unmistakable bob capriciously complements an androgynously seductive performance in one film, and an adorably impish turn in another. When it came to the star’s chocolate-hazelnut eyes, different auteurs saw different characters. Binoche’s kinetic, comic abilities sparkle in early, dynamic collaborations with Leos Carax, who teases making a generic Pierrot of her with shaving cream and a white eye patch. There is, however, no way to blanch Binoche. Krzysztof Kieślowski, for his part, almost drowns her with shimmering blues, but it only surfaces her capacity for profound melancholy. After Binoche took Best Actress at Venice in 1994, a César and an Oscar® followed, and later prestigious prizes from Berlin and Cannes. Through the new millennium, Binoche had top billing in works by Claire Denis, Olivier Assayas, Abbas Kiarostami, Michael Haneke, and countless other acclaimed auteurs worldwide. Now in her fourth decade on screen, she punctuates metafictional and extreme visions with the stilettoed point of her star persona for some directors; for others, she stirs sumptuous passion into period dramas and romances with her exquisite, easy sensuality. Woman of a thousand faces, certainly—and a face that launched a thousand cinephiles too.

title: Viewfinder
short_synopsis: A globetrotting survey of bold storytelling and essential perspectives on the world.
long_synopsis: A globetrotting survey of bold storytelling and essential perspectives on the world.

long_synopsis: New short films by some of the best up-and-coming as well as established filmmakers and artists.

title: Debuts
short_synopsis: A celebration of first features that hail the arrival of exciting directors.
long_synopsis: A celebration of first features that hail the arrival of exciting directors.

title: Adaptations
short_synopsis: The adaptation of pre-existing texts has long been a foundation of film art, as directors make fascinating choices to reshape existing work, creating their own imprint while remaining in conversation with the original. To explore a vast basis of storytelling, we offer this eclectic ongoing series.
long_synopsis: Since its inception, cinema has been in dialogue with, incorporated, and even cannibalized other art forms, and the immense question of adaptation has long been a foundation of film art. Filmmakers make fascinating choices by choosing to reshape an existing work, creating their own imprint while remaining in conversation with the original. To explore this vast, challenging basis of storytelling, we offer this eclectic ongoing series showcasing films, new and old, which vary in their relationship to the source material, from faithful retellings to wildly loose inspirations.

title: Ethnographic Fictions:
subtitle: Spotlight on Laura Huertas Millán
full_title: Ethnographic Fictions: Spotlight on Laura Huertas Millán
short_synopsis: Even if Colombia-born, France-based artist filmmaker Laura Huertas Millán has so far only worked with short and medium-length formats, she has a remarkably sharp, meaningful body of work. MUBI is proud to present a double bill of two of her “ethnographic fictions”, a term borrowed from pioneer Je…
long_synopsis: Even if Colombia-born, France-based artist filmmaker Laura Huertas Millán has so far only worked with short and medium-length formats, she has a remarkably sharp, meaningful body of work. MUBI is proud to present a double bill of two of her “ethnographic fictions”, a term borrowed from pioneer Jean Rouch by Huertas Millán after spending a year at the prestigious Sensory Ethnography Lab of Harvard University (Leviathan, Manakamana) in order to frame the tension at the heart of her films: a beguiling negotiation between the documentary gaze and the staging of reality, mediated by an anthropological approach to images. These works examine the heritage of colonialism in contemporary societies through a variety of lenses and languages, and navigate both familiar territory and the so-called otherness with the same rigour, taking the tradition of ethnography to unexpected places. Revolving around female figures and two different matriarchal structures, Black Sun and La Libertad explore new forms of resilience while quietly subverting our ideas of freedom and independence.

title: Festival Focus:
subtitle: Rotterdam
short_synopsis: A constant vanguard for daring, risk-taking cinema, the lauded International Film Festival Rotterdam (IFFR) arrives every January with hundreds of film premieres from around the world. We are proud to exclusively present a number of special gems from recent years.
long_synopsis: A constant vanguard for daring, risk-taking cinema, the International Film Festival Rotterdam (IFFR) is as lauded as it is sprawling, presenting hundreds of film premieres from around the world each January. We are proud to exclusively present a number of special gems from recent years.

title: Krzysztof Kieślowski:
subtitle: Three Colors Trilogy
short_synopsis: Krzysztof Kieślowski’s ambitious and interconnected trilogy deciphers the mysteries of the human condition. Starring Juliette Binoche, Julie Delpy and Irène Jacob, the Three Colors films attentively investigate the behaviors of everyday characters in stories of death, fate, chance, and love.
long_synopsis: One of the most important European auteurs of the late 20th century, Krzysztof Kieślowski delivered an astonishing affirmation of life and art with his final three features, which are among the most internationally acclaimed and commercially successful arthouse releases of the 1990s. These transcendental and existentialist films decipher the mysteries of the human condition, through closely and attentively investigating the behaviors and actions of everyday characters. Named after the three colors of the French flag, and inspired by the three universal values of the national motto—freedom, equality and fraternity—this interconnected trilogy became a source of admiration and inspiration for cinephiles around the world. The final film, Three Colors: Red, even picked up three Oscar® nominations, including Best Director for Kieślowski. The Three Colors trilogy explores stories of death and rebirth, fate, chance and (sometimes twisted) love. Both the locations—France, Switzerland and Kieślowski’s native Poland—and considerably different approaches to atmosphere, cinematography and sound make each part distinctive, which makes the little ways in which the film’s stories connect all the more compelling. Led by Juliette Binoche (Blue), Zbigniew Zamachowski (White) and Irène Jacob (Red), the trilogy also features memorable supporting turns from Julie Delpy and the legendary Jean-Louis Trintignant.

title: Restored By Nicolas Winding Refn
short_synopsis: “It was a myth… No one had ever seen it.” —Nicolas Winding Refn on The Nest of the Cuckoo Birds Welcome to the world of byNWR AN UNADULTERATED CULTURAL EXPRESSWAY FOR THE ARTS Born from Nicolas Winding Refn’s passion for the rare, the forgotten and the unknown, byNWR breathes new …
long_synopsis: “It was a myth… No one had ever seen it.” —Nicolas Winding Refn on The Nest of the Cuckoo Birds Welcome to the world of byNWR AN UNADULTERATED CULTURAL EXPRESSWAY FOR THE ARTS Born from Nicolas Winding Refn’s passion for the rare, the forgotten and the unknown, byNWR breathes new life into the culturally intriguing and influential. Home to cultural ephemera created by an array of Guest Editors and contributors, our beautifully restored and revived cinematic gems inspire a world of original content, where we share, enjoy and look to the future with hope, prosperity and the idea that culture is for everyone.

title: American Indies
short_synopsis: American low-budget, outside-the-mainstream films seem to be populating our watchlists today more than ever. Only a few of them, nevertheless, might truly stand out as memorable ones. In face of such recent overabundance, we’re thrilled to present a selection of American indies that stand out …
long_synopsis: American low-budget, outside-the-mainstream films seem to be populating our watchlists today more than ever. Only a few of them, nevertheless, might truly stand out as memorable ones. In face of such recent overabundance, we’re thrilled to present a selection of American indies that stand out as some of our own favorites—all proving how adventurous and refreshingly bold storytelling can be.

title: And the Oscar Goes to...
short_synopsis: Star-studded and Oscar®-nominated—some winners, some losers—the films here are among the choicest cuts from the Night of a Thousand Stars. It’s the perfect opportunity to brush up on your Oscar® trivia, fill some viewing gaps, or convince yourself again that a certain film or performance was robbed.
long_synopsis: Oscar® night. A time for winners and losers, tears and cheers. A night of sweeps, surprises, and foregone conclusions. Every year viewers and attendees reenact a dramatic formula nearly a century in the making: tuxes are pressed, champagne poured, and ballots filled at home. It’s a given that someone’s going to cry, and some great films will be going home empty-handed. Think you know your Oscar® history? MUBI presents a selection of winners and nominees from Oscars® past, the perfect opportunity to brush up on your awards trivia, fill some of those viewing gaps, or convince yourself again that a certain film or performance was robbed. Star-studded and Oscar®-nominated, the films here are some of the choice cuts from the Night of a Thousand Stars…

title: First Films First
short_synopsis: A director’s first film can provide the roadmap for an entire oeuvre. Our series of directorial debuts revisits the films that launched the careers of some of cinema’s finest auteurs.
long_synopsis: A director’s first film can provide the roadmap for an entire oeuvre. Our series of directorial debuts revisits the films that launched the careers of some of cinema’s finest auteurs.

title: It's About Time:
subtitle: The Cinema of Lav Diaz
full_title: It's About Time: The Cinema of Lav Diaz
short_synopsis: This spotlight focuses on the visionary films of renowned Filipino auteur Lav Diaz. His sprawling sagas of the Philippines’ tumultuous recent history and beleaguered but strong-willed peoples are epic in scope but bracingly intimate and direct in style. No one makes movies quite like him.
long_synopsis: This spotlight focuses on the visionary films of renowned Filipino auteur Lav Diaz. Winner of Locarno’s Golden Leopard, and the Venice Orizzonti and Berlinale’s Alfred Bauer awards, his sprawling sagas of the Philippines’ tumultuous recent history and beleaguered but strong-willed and passionate peoples are epic in scope but bracingly intimate and direct in style. Challenging notions of storytelling and how cinema gives images and sound to stories unseen and voices unheard, no one makes movies like him.

title: American Outsider:
subtitle: The Films of Kelly Reichardt
full_title: American Outsider:  The Films of Kelly Reichardt
short_synopsis: Kelly Reichardt is, quite simply, one of the most distinctive voices in independent cinema, often reframing how we see North America on film. Immersed in small gestures of ambivalence and resistance, her terrains of radical restlessness open up new ways of looking at time, space, and life itself.
long_synopsis: Kelly Reichardt is one of the most distinctive voices in American independent film. In her cinema of outsiders, she imbues North American landscapes not with progress, but frustration and restlessness. Those terrains encompass forest-green trails, stretches of deserted land, and desolate parking lots, where transient characters abound, struggling to reconcile their needs with an uncaring environment. Often working with a core group of collaborators, Reichardt finds a creative kinship in those who allow for her wry humor, her unsentimental vision. Many of her films not only dissect but also reimagine the worn-out iconography of American modernity. In all of this, she sidesteps histrionics, opting instead for quiet rumination, small gestures of resistance and ambivalence, and a stirring stillness that is potent in its maturity and emotional sensitivity. Much has been written about long takes in discussions of Reichardt’s minimalism. In that elongated attention to the quotidian and the minor, she is subtly political. These scenes invite us to reconsider time, to unlatch it from capitalist order. Immersion in Reichardt’s cinema of wandering opens up rare things: new ways of looking at time, space, and life itself.
